user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous call (up, 0.45) was correct in direction: fed_assets rose again to 8,860,485 from 8,867,834 one observation back — actually the latest print (8,860,485) is slightly below the prior observation (8,867,834), a small dip, but the broader 4-week and 13-week changes remain strongly positive (+103,025 and +304,304 respectively), and the level sits essentially at the 52-week high (only 7,349 below it). The policy language confirms continued net asset purchases, but at a tapering pace: Treasury purchases cut from $80B/month equivalent path down to $20B/month and MBS to $10B/month beginning [date] following a prior cut from $40B/$20B. This is still QE — assets are still being added, not drained — but the pace of expansion is decelerating sharply toward zero. Given tapering is explicit and nearly complete, the balance sheet is still growing but at a much slower rate, and the single most recent observation ticked down slightly, which could be noise or the first sign of the taper's effect. Net acquisition pace is small and shrinking, consistent with expansion continuing through month-end but at a diminishing rate, not yet flat or reversing.
Would change its mind if: A subsequent month-over-month decline in fed_assets, or language announcing purchases reduced to zero/runoff, would flip this to flat or down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "flat" call at 0.35 conviction was wrong: the spread has now broken decisively below 0.70, hitting a new 12-month low of 0.61, exactly the falsifier condition I set for renewed flattening. The last three readings (0.63, 0.63, 0.61) show flattening momentum has not stalled, with slope_change_21d at -0.18pp and slope_change_63d at -0.46pp both confirming a persistent, accelerating flattening trend rather than noise. The spread sits at slope_range_position 0.00 — the very bottom of its 12-month range — with slope_distance_from_flat down to just 0.61pp, meaning inversion risk is now much closer. Policy language has shifted hawkish: guidance moved from tapering-in-progress to "soon appropriate to raise the target range," which historically compresses front-end-to-belly spreads faster than the long end, consistent with the observed flattening. The 10s30s slope at +0.32pp remains modestly positive, suggesting the flattening pressure is concentrated in the 2s10s segment rather than a broad long-end selloff. With no signs of stabilization in the last three prints and hawkish rhetoric intensifying, I expect continued flattening into month-end, though the pace of decline may moderate somewhat given how compressed the spread already is.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 0.70 sustained for 2+ readings, or slope_change_21d turning positive, would signal flattening has stalled.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_distance_from_flat, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has essentially stabilized after its earlier tightening drift. Over the last 13 weeks the index rose modestly (+0.08) from very loose levels, but the most recent 4 weeks show a slight reversal (-0.01), with the last several prints clustered tightly between -0.54 and -0.56. The latest value (-0.55) sits right at the middle of this recent range, and the range_position of 0.90 shows we're near the high (tighter) end of the 52-week span, but momentum has flattened rather than continuing to build. My prior flat call was directionally correct — the anticipated resumption of a clear multi-week rise did not materialize, nor did a drop back toward -0.60. The series is oscillating in a narrow band with no clear trend. Policy language remains unchanged, describing conditions as "accommodative," consistent with the very negative distance-from-zero reading (-0.55), but this is stale boilerplate and not informative for the one-month change. Given the flattening momentum and lack of a decisive directional push in either the measurements or the policy text, I maintain a flat call, with slightly higher conviction than before since the range has now held for several consecutive weeks without breaking.
Would change its mind if: A break below -0.60 (renewed easing) or a sustained multi-week climb toward -0.50 or higher (renewed tightening) would change this call.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 10 breakevens (2.46→2.44) show stabilization after an early dip, not a renewed uptrend. The series bottomed near 2.33 mid-window, rebounded to 2.38-2.44, and has now printed flat for two consecutive readings (2.44, 2.44). My prior "up" call was wrong in direction of magnitude — the 21d and 63d changes are both modestly negative (-0.14, -0.13 pp), showing the medium-term trend is still net down from where it was a month/quarter ago, even though the most recent few prints have leveled off. The 252d change (+0.31pp) shows expectations remain well above where they were a year ago, and the range position (0.51) sits right in the middle of the 12-month band, arguing against a strong directional signal either way. Distance from target remains firmly above 2% (+0.44pp), consistent with expectations anchored moderately above objective, but that's a level story, not a momentum one. Given the flattening in the last two prints after the dip, and negative but decelerating short/medium-term momentum, I don't see strong evidence for continued upward drift. The most defensible call now is essentially flat, with a slight lean toward stabilization rather than a resumed rise.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 2.50 or below 2.35 sustained over multiple observations would confirm a genuine directional move rather than range-bound flatness.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.62
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y rose further to 7.56% from 7.17%, and core PCE y/y climbed to 5.21% from 4.89%, extending an unbroken multi-month acceleration. The falsifier I set (two consecutive sub-0.3% mom prints plus core PCE turning down) did not occur — mom actually reaccelerated: 0.86 → 0.69 → 0.60, still well above the 0.72% 3-month average and far above any level consistent with 2% annualized inflation. The 3m annualized headline pace (8.95%) is running hotter than the 12m y/y rate (7.56%), and core 3m annualized (6.64%) likewise exceeds its own y/y print, meaning momentum is not decelerating even though the single latest mom reading (0.60) is modestly below the immediately preceding two. Policy language confirms this isn't yet seen as easing: officials shifted from calling imbalances "transitory" to acknowledging inflation "has exceeded 2 percent for some time," and began tapering asset purchases — a clear acknowledgment of sustained overheating. Core is running far above target (+3.21pp) and the range position is at the top of its 12-month band (1.00). While the deceleration in mom from 0.86 to 0.60 is a mild yellow flag worth watching, it's not enough on its own to break the trend given still-elevated 3m annualized rates and rising y/y in both series. I still expect headline CPI y/y to rise next month.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.3% combined with core PCE y/y turning down and 3m annualized headline falling below the 12m y/y rate.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.00% from 3.90%, an uptick of 0.10pp after a long, steep decline (-2.40pp over 12 months). This confirms my prior falsifier condition — I said a print at or above 4.00-4.10% would signal stalling, and that's exactly what happened. The unemployment rate is now essentially at the 3m average (4.00%) and just 0.10pp above the 12-month low, so the Sahm-gap remains tiny (+0.10pp) and doesn't yet indicate a genuine trend reversal or rapid deterioration. However, the multi-month streak of consecutive declines has clearly broken — this is the first uptick after six straight prints of falling or flat readings. Policy language still describes "substantial" declines and "solid" job gains, but that language reflects the trend through the prior prints, not this newest uptick, and is now being used to justify tightening (tapering asset purchases), consistent with labor conditions being assessed as near maximum employment rather than still loosening rapidly. Given the level is at the natural rate (distance_from_natural ≈ 0) and the increase is small but real, I read this as a stalling of the tightening trend rather than a resumption of loosening — a flat-to-slightly-up call with modest conviction, not a confident reversal.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.20% or a Sahm-gap exceeding 0.50pp would confirm a genuine loosening trend rather than a stall near the natural rate.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, sahm_gap, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_3m_avg

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is validated: dgs10 has climbed from 1.75 to 1.79 recently after a dip, with 21d change +0.27pp and 63d +0.22pp, both positive. The 12-month range position sits at 0.90, meaning the yield is near the top of its trailing range, and it's 0.70pp above its 12m low versus only 0.08pp below the 12m high. The 252d change of +0.68pp confirms a strong sustained uptrend over the past year. The 10s30s slope of +0.32pp is not inverted, consistent with continued long-end pressure rather than curve flattening from flight-to-quality. Policy language confirms continued tapering of Treasury purchases (now down to $20bn/month from $40bn), which reduces central bank demand for long-end paper and should keep upward pressure on term premium via reduced balance-sheet support. This is a incremental continuation of the previously announced taper, not a reversal, so it reinforces rather than changes the up call. Momentum has been choppy short-term (1.85→1.81→1.78→1.79) but the broader trend and level near range highs support continued modest upward pressure into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-session drop below 1.70 with negative 21d momentum would signal easing term-premium pressure and reverse this call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace and composition of future runoff/taper decisions directly drive long-end supply pressure.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment could trigger flight-to-quality that would offset taper-driven upward pressure.

arbitrated.notes: The front-end call rests on labor and inflation, and this meeting the two are pulling in opposite directions but with very different data freshness and momentum. Inflation (formed 16 days ago, conviction 0.62) shows headline CPI y/y accelerating to 7.56% and core PCE to 5.21%, with 3m annualized rates still running hotter than y/y in both series — momentum has not broken despite a modest mom deceleration (0.86→0.69→0.60). This is a live, high-impact read: hot and accelerating inflation is exactly what forces the Fed to price more near-term tightening, and policy language has already shifted to acknowledging inflation "exceeded 2 percent for some time." Labor (formed 23 days ago, conviction 0.35) shows unemployment ticking up to 4.00% from 3.90%, breaking a long declining streak, but the analyst itself frames this as a "stall near the natural rate," not a genuine loosening — Sahm-gap is trivially small. This is a weak, tentative signal on a driver that's already near its cyclical low, and it's staler than the inflation read. Given inflation is the fresher, higher-conviction, and higher-market-impact driver here, and labor is explicitly framed as inconclusive stalling rather than reversal, the reaction-function balance still tilts toward MORE near-term tightening being priced, consistent with my prior stance. I'm not moving off that read — nothing here overturns it, inflation reaffirms it, labor is too weak/stale to offset it. Financial conditions and balance sheet remain the constraint, not the driver: FC is flat/range-bound and balance sheet, while still growing, is decelerating exactly as already announced — both are anticipated and priced, not repricing catalysts. I hold a front-end position expressing continued upward pressure on near-term rate expectations, modestly steeper within 2y to reflect that inflation is the more front-loaded pressure while labor stalling caps the very front end slightly.

user_prompt:
Your previous meeting
  You called: balance_sheet +0.25, financial_conditions +0.00, inflation +0.62, labor_tightness +0.05
  The position you are carrying: DGS2 +0.70, DGS3MO -0.30 (conviction 0.60).
  You said this read would flip if: A CPI/core PCE print showing mom below 0.3% and 3m annualized falling under y/y, combined with unemployment breaking above 4.20%, would flip this toward less tightening priced.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print confirms continued balance-sheet expansion: total assets rose to 8,928,129 from 8,911,033, a fresh 52-week high, consistent with my prior "up" call, which was correct. The 4-week change (+67,644) and 13-week change (+246,358, +2.84%) both remain positive, though the pace is clearly decelerating — the taper is visibly working, with monthly purchase caps stepping down from $80bn+ combined to just $30bn combined (Treasuries $20bn + MBS $10bn) starting [date] down from $40bn/$20bn the prior month. This is still net QE, just at a shrinking rate. With purchases still positive but being halved again, assets should keep rising into month-end, but by a smaller increment than the trailing 4-week pace suggests, since the new lower monthly pace only partially reflects in the data shown. No language yet signals a move to zero purchases or runoff, so I expect continued (slowing) growth in assets, not a reversal. Reserve creation continues, just at a decelerating clip, which is the "why" behind the still-positive but flattening curve of increases.
Would change its mind if: A month-over-month decline in fed_assets, or language announcing an end to purchases/shift to runoff, would flip this to flat or down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: slope fell from 0.42 to 0.39, continuing the flattening trend. The 2s10s spread sits at 0.39pp, essentially at its 12-month low (0.38) with range_position of just 0.01 — meaning the curve is near the flattest point of the past year. All momentum measures confirm this: 21d change -0.24, 63d change -0.59, 252d change -0.91, all negative and accelerating over longer windows. The policy language has shifted decisively hawkish — from tapering to an explicit signal that a hike is imminent ("expects it will soon be appropriate to raise the target range"), with asset purchases ending in early [date] . This front-loads tightening expectations, pressuring the front end (2Y) higher relative to the long end, which is the classic mechanism for flattening. The 10s30s slope at 0.34 shows the long end itself is not steepening either, consistent with a broad-based flattening rather than a front-end-only phenomenon. With the spread already pinned near cycle lows and hawkish rhetoric intensifying rather than stabilizing, there is no evidence of a rebound — my stated falsifier (sustained readings above 0.70, or 21d turning positive) has not been met. I expect continued modest flattening or stabilization at a compressed level.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 0.55 sustained for 2+ readings, or slope_change_21d turning positive, would signal flattening has stalled or reversed.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI has moved from -0.55 to -0.47 over the last several weeks, a clear and steady rise. The 4-week change (+0.09) and 13-week change (+0.10) are consistent with each other, indicating this is not noise but a sustained trend of tightening momentum. The index is now sitting at its 52-week high, with range_position at 1.00 - the top of its recent band - confirming this is the tightest reading in the observed window, even though the level (-0.47) remains below the zero average, so conditions are still net accommodative in absolute terms. My prior flat call was wrong: at that point the climb toward -0.50 was already underway and has now cleared through that threshold, which I had flagged as my trigger to reverse. The steady, monotonic [date] upward over the last several observations (particularly the last 4: -0.54, -0.52, -0.49, -0.47) shows accelerating tightening momentum rather than stabilization. Policy language remains static and accommodative-oriented, but the measurement itself is the graded signal and it points unambiguously higher. I now call this up with good conviction given the consistency across both short and medium windows.

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been invalidated: breakeven_10y broke decisively above 2.50 and has continued climbing to 2.62, the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.77, only 0.14pp below the 12m high of 2.76). The last two readings (2.54→2.62) show acceleration, not consolidation, and the 21-day change (+0.22pp) is now much larger than the 63-day change (+0.07pp), indicating the move is recent and gaining pace rather than fading. The 252-day change (+0.47pp) confirms a sustained uptrend over the year, not noise. Distance from the 2% objective is now +0.62pp, well above where it sat a few observations ago, suggesting market-implied inflation expectations are becoming increasingly unanchored to the upside relative to target. With price action pushing to fresh highs within the range and momentum building rather than decelerating, the evidence supports continuation over reversion. I was wrong to stay flat last time — the multi-observation break above 2.50 that I set as my falsifier has now occurred emphatically. I'll shift to a clear "up" call, tempered only by the fact that breakevens are near the top of their historical range and could see some mean-reversion resistance there.
Would change its mind if: A pullback below 2.50 sustained over 2-3 observations, or a flattening of the 21-day change toward zero, would signal the uptrend has stalled.

Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)

Core PCE climbed again to 5.36% from 5.21%, still rising monotonically for 13 straight readings with no sign of plateauing. The Sahm gap is essentially nil (+0.03pp), confirming no loosening signal whatsoever. The 3m change (-0.20pp) and 6m change (-1.20pp) both show continued tightening, not stabilization. My prior "flat" call was proven wrong by this data — the market kept tightening rather than stalling near the natural rate. The level is now 0.10pp below my assumed natural rate anchor of 4%, indicating a historically tight labor market with no sign of turning. Policy language also confirms this: statements shifted from acknowledging "further improvement in the labor market" to declaring a "strong labor market" as justification for raising rates soon — language consistent with a labor market at or beyond full employment, not one showing cracks. Momentum has been consistently downward (declining unemployment) for months. Absent a clear reversal signal, the most likely near-term outcome is continued flatness at this very low level, with a modest risk of a further tick down given the persistent trend, rather than any reversal upward.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.10% or a Sahm-gap exceeding 0.30pp would signal a genuine turn toward loosening.

Given the tapering continues and the level remains high in its range, I lean toward the drift resuming upward over the next month, but the recent dip and flat 21d change temper confidence. This is a lean, not a strong call.
Would change its mind if: A confirmed multi-session move below 1.75 with negative 21d momentum and flattening 30s10s slope would indicate the term-premium uptrend has genuinely reversed.

arbitrated.notes:
Labor and inflation are the two reaction-function drivers I own, and both have hardened since last meeting rather than softened. Inflation: headline CPI accelerated to 7.94%, core PCE to 5.36% (13th straight monthly rise), 3m annualized running hotter than y/y on both measures, and policy language moved from "some time above 2%" to "well above 2%" with tightening imminent. This is a clear, non-stale, high-conviction "up" call with no offsetting signal — my prior falsifier (sub-0.3% MoM, core PCE turning down) was not met; the opposite happened. Labor: unemployment ticked to a fresh 12-month low (3.90%), Sahm gap near nil, 3m/6m changes both show continued tightening, and policy language shifted to "strong labor market" framing. The analyst still labels this "flat" but the underlying data and direction are unambiguously tightening (falling unemployment = tighter labor), so I read this as a modest tightening signal in the reaction function even though the headline conviction is only 0.30 — this is a live, un-stale read, not something to discount for staleness.

There is no genuine labor-loosening vs hot-inflation tension here — both drivers point the same way: more near-term tightening priced, not less. Financial conditions (up, tightening) and balance sheet (still expanding, but decelerating) are consistent with the "constraint" already loosely priced and don't override the reaction-function read; they confirm rather than contradict it. Curve flattening and term premium moves are outside my mandate but corroborate that markets are front-loading hikes, which is directionally consistent with my call.

Nothing in this panel undercuts my prior thesis — if anything it strengthens it. I hold the position: long front-end yields via 2y, funded short 3-month, reflecting more near-term tightening priced with the curve front-loading hikes into the 2y point.

Labor conviction from the analyst itself is low (0.30); a soft print could quickly reverse the tightening read.

why.inflation: CPI/core PCE both accelerating, 3m annualized above y/y, hawkish language shift — clear up call, unchanged from prior meeting.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment fell to fresh 12m low (tighter labor, own-measure conviction low per analyst but direction is tightening).

With no evidence of momentum reversal, deceleration, or claims data suggesting a inflection, I lean toward continued tightness. However, core PCE y/y is still creeping up at the margin (5.60 → 5.59, essentially flat at a cycle high) and core_3m_annualized (5.48%) remains well above the 2% target, so underlying persistence has not clearly broken. Given headline's single-month deceleration plus the sharply lower MoM print, I lean toward another flat-to-lower headline print next month, but conviction is capped because core momentum has not turned and one data point does not confirm a trend.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print reaccelerating above 0.7% combined with core PCE y/y resuming its climb would reverse this softening lean.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, headline_3m_annualized, core_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print holds at 3.70%, matching the prior month and sitting at the 12-month low. My prior call for a further decline did not materialize this month—unemployment was flat rather than down—so that call was too aggressive given the rate had already compressed to its floor. Momentum indicators confirm the market is no longer loosening in the labor-tightness sense but also isn't tightening further: unrate_change_3m (-0.30pp) and change_6m (-0.80pp) reflect the earlier trend, not the last month's flat print. Sahm_gap is a negligible +0.07pp, far from the 0.30+ threshold that would signal a genuine turning point toward loosening. Range position at 0.00 (right at the 12-month low) with distance from natural rate at -0.30pp shows the labor market is historically tight and has been for some time, but the deceleration in further improvement (two consecutive 3.70-3.90% readings) suggests the rate of tightening has plateaued. Policy language still describes a "strong" labor market with "solid"/"strong" job gains, consistent with no loosening signal yet. Absent a fresh catalyst, the most defensible call is flat, not down — the low has plausibly been reached and further declines are not evidenced by the recent flat trajectory.
Would change its mind if: A print below 3.70% (new low, resuming tightening) or above 3.90-4.00% (reversal toward loosening) would overturn this flat call.
Leaned on: unrate_3m_avg, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_change_3m

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield printed 2.89 last, near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.98, only 0.04pp below the 12m high of 2.93). Momentum remains firmly upward: +0.54pp over 21d, +1.11pp over 63d, +1.26pp over 252d — a persistent, accelerating climb rather than a stalling one. The prior "up" call at 0.40 conviction was correct: yields continued rising from 2.85 to 2.89 with no sign of the reversal I specified (no break below 2.10, no negative momentum, no flattening 30s10s). The 30s10s slope is positive (+0.07pp) and 10s2s is positive (+0.19pp), so the curve is not inverting at the long end, consistent with continued upward pressure rather than flight-to-quality. Policy language now explicitly flags plans to begin reducing balance-sheet holdings "at a coming meeting" — this is a new hawkish shift versus the prior statement's still-expanding purchase program, and balance-sheet runoff is a structural driver that lifts long-end term premium. Combined with the yield sitting at a fresh 12-month high and strong multi-horizon momentum, the evidence supports continuation of the uptrend into month-end, though the level is stretched which tempers how much further conviction I place on it.
Would change its mind if: A confirmed multi-session move back toward 2.10-2.30 with negative 21d momentum and a flattening/inverting 30s10s slope would signal the uptrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_12m_high, slope_30s10s, slope_10s2s

arbitrated.notes: The core reaction-function pair has softened at the margin but not reversed. Labor: unemployment holds flat at 3.70%, a 12-month low, with Sahm gap negligible — the market is still historically tight, but the pace of further tightening has plateaued (flat vs. my prior -0.25 lean toward further tightening). That's a genuine, if modest, downgrade to my labor conviction — I move from -0.25 toward something closer to flat, since the loosening I expected hasn't shown up and the tight print itself keeps the Fed hawkish. Inflation: headline CPI y/y ticked down for the first time in 13 months (8.57→8.23) and MoM was soft (0.31%, well under trailing 3m average), partially triggering my own falsifier. But core PCE y/y is still creeping up at a cycle high and core 3m annualized (5.48%) sits far above target — the persistence the Fed actually reacts to has not broken. Inflation expectations (breakeven 10y) bounced back near 12-month highs, undercutting the "disinflation is real" story and suggesting the market itself isn't pricing a genuine turn. So headline cooled, but the reaction-function-relevant core/expectations picture didn't. Financial conditions continue drifting toward tighter but are decelerating and still deeply accommodative in level — a constraint, not a driver, and not enough alone to add hawkish weight. Balance sheet is plateauing, consistent with prior read, non-directional for near-term policy pricing. Net: my prior CPI-cooling+unemployment-break falsifier partially triggered (headline soft) but the unemployment leg (needs >4.20%) did not — still at 3.70%. That means the flip condition is NOT satisfied. I retain a modest near-term tightening lean but trim conviction given the headline softening and labor plateau, rather than fully reversing.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation shows a sharp drop to 8,914,281 from 8,945,898 the prior week, a decline of roughly -31,600 $M in a single week — the largest weekly move in this series. This follows a choppy but roughly flat 13-week stretch (assets_13w_avg ~8,939,186, pct_change_13w only -0.16%), so my prior "flat" call was reasonable through most of the window but the newest print is the first clear break lower. Critically, the policy language has now shifted from merely "expects to begin" runoff to "decided to begin" runoff with a specific start date and an issued implementation plan. That is exactly the trigger I flagged previously as the condition for changing my mind. Combined with the sudden step-down in assets and the level now sitting furthest below the 52-week high (-51,206), this looks like the start of active balance-sheet runoff rather than noise. One week of decline plus a confirmed policy commitment is not yet a long trend, so I won't go to extreme conviction, but the evidence now clearly favors continued decline into next month-end as the caps take effect.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets over the next few weekly prints, or statement language walking back/delaying the runoff start, would overturn this call.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.

  The previous "down" call was wrong: the last observation (0.32) is a fresh high in the 10-print series, up from 0.27 and 0.29 in the prior two prints, and slope_change_21d is now solidly positive (+0.13pp). That said, slope_change_63d is still negative (-0.09pp) and slope_change_252d is deeply negative (-1.15pp), showing the dominant trend over the past year has been flattening from a much steeper starting point (12m high +1.47pp) toward near-flat (12m low -0.05pp). The spread sits at only 0.24 of its 12-month range — still historically low/inverted-adjacent, well below the midpoint. However, the most recent three readings (0.27, 0.29, 0.32) show a consistent short-term uptick, and the Fed continuing on its hiking path (raising to 3/4-1%) with front-end (dgs2 2.53%) still below long end (dgs10 2.85%) suggests the front end has room to keep rising alongside, but the recent short-term momentum favors mild steepening. This is a modest reversal signal, not yet the "three consecutive closes above 0.25 with 63d turning positive" threshold I set, since 63d is still negative. I'll shift to a slight up lean but with low-moderate conviction given the mixed short vs long trend signals.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below 0.25pp or slope_change_63d turning more negative would confirm the flattening trend is resuming, not a genuine steepening.

Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has moved steadily and monotonically higher over the last 13 weeks, from -0.44 to -0.28, with the most recent reading now sitting at the 52-week high (range_position = 1.00). The 4-week change (+0.07) and 13-week change (+0.19) both confirm sustained upward momentum with no pullback at any point in the series — every single weekly print has been flat-to-higher. My prior call (up, 0.35) was correct: conditions continued tightening rather than reversing, and the falsifier I set (a drop below -0.39 or two consecutive declines) did not trigger — quite the opposite, the trend accelerated toward the top of its range. Index level remains negative (-0.28), so conditions are still looser than the historical average in absolute terms, but the direction of travel is unambiguously toward tighter conditions. Given the persistence and consistency of this trend (13 consecutive weeks without a single down-week), I have more confidence now than at the last release that this is a genuine tightening trend rather than noise. Barring a sharp reversal, the momentum favors continuation into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print below -0.30 (a reversal of the last move) or two consecutive weekly declines would signal the tightening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_above_52w_low

Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (up, 0.35) was wrong: breakeven_10y has been drifting sideways-to-slightly-up off a low base near 2.55-2.60, not resuming an uptrend toward higher levels from 2.85+. The falsifier I set (sustained drop below 2.85) is moot since the series never approached that level in this window — I mis-scaled the prior read. Looking at the actual data: the series fell sharply from 2.71/2.72 to a 2.55-2.60 range (21d change -0.24pp), then has stabilized and ticked up modestly over the last four prints (2.55→2.56→2.62→2.63→2.64), with 63d change flat (+0.02pp) and 252d change still positive (+0.22pp). The most recent three observations (2.62, 2.63, 2.64) show a small consistent uptick, which is the freshest signal available and outweighs the older 21d drop.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "down" call was wrong: headline CPI y/y rose again to 8.54% and the MoM print jumped to 0.95%, well above the 0.7% threshold I set as a reversal trigger. Headline 3m annualized momentum is 9.91%, still hot, and headline_yoy_change_3m is +0.60pp — accelerating, not decelerating. Core PCE y/y, the better persistence read, ticked down slightly to 5.35% from 5.59-5.60%, and core_yoy_change_3m is essentially flat (-0.01pp), suggesting core momentum may be plateauing near a high level rather than continuing to climb. This is a genuine headline/core divergence: headline is being pushed by energy/food volatility (large MoM swing after a soft 0.31% print), while core suggests the underlying trend is very elevated but not accelerating further. Given the explicit one-month horizon is for headline CPI, and the latest MoM (0.95%) is stronger than the trailing 3m average (0.79%) and the outgoing print, the near-term momentum favors another firm print. Base effects are hard to judge without knowing the year-ago comparison month, but the sequential acceleration in MoM data is the dominant signal here. I lean toward a further modest increase or at least a high, sticky reading, reversing my prior softening call.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print below ~0.4% combined with core PCE y/y continuing to fall would confirm a genuine turn rather than noise.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print of 3.60% is a new 12-month low, undercutting the prior floor of 3.70% and confirming that my previous "flat" call was wrong — the market resumed tightening rather than stabilizing. The unemployment rate has now fallen for three straight readings (3.70, 3.70, 3.60) after a brief uptick to 3.90-4.00%, and the 3m/6m/12m changes are all negative (-0.30, -0.50, -2.20 pp), showing sustained downward momentum in unemployment. The level is 0.40pp below the ~4% natural rate estimate, and the Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) is a negligible +0.07pp, meaning we are sitting essentially at the cycle low with no sign of the sharp-rise pattern that would signal loosening. Policy language reinforces this, upgrading "strong" job gains to "robust" and reiterating substantial declines in unemployment. There is no evidence in the measurements of any turn — no rise off the low, no deceleration in the pace of decline. Given the consistent multi-horizon negative changes and a fresh cycle low, the near-term signal favors continued tightness or at worst stabilization at a very low level. I'll lean toward another flat-to-slightly-down reading next month, but with the recognition that at levels this low, mean-reversion risk rises.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70-3.80% would signal a turn toward loosening; a print at or below 3.60% would confirm continued tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call largely played out: the level has climbed from the high-2s into the current 2.85, with 63-day and 252-day changes of +1.13pp and +1.24pp respectively, confirming a strong multi-quarter uptrend. Range position sits at 0.86, meaning the yield is near the top of its 12-month band, and the 12m low/high (1.19%/3.12%) frame just how far this move has traveled. The most recent print ticked up to 2.85 from 2.74 the prior observation, reversing a brief dip, so the short-run trajectory remains upward even though the 21-day change is slightly negative (-0.04pp), indicating some recent stalling/consolidation rather than reversal. The 30s10s slope is a modestly positive +0.22pp, showing no inversion signal that would suggest the long end is being bid down relative to the ultra-long end. Policy language now confirms actual commencement of balance-sheet runoff (Treasury and MBS holdings reduction), replacing forward-guidance language with an operational start date — this is a concrete supply-side headwind for the long end, reinforcing higher term premium. Combined with the still-elevated inflation language, the environment continues to favor higher long-end yields. I maintain an "up" call, though tempered slightly given the flat 21-day momentum suggesting the pace of ascent may be moderating even as the level itself sits near cycle highs.
Would change its mind if: A confirmed multi-session move back toward 2.10-2.30 with negative 21d momentum and a flattening/inverting 30s10s slope would signal the uptrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes:
Labor and inflation are the two reaction-function drivers I actually weight, and both point the same direction I held last time, only more so. Labor: unemployment printed a fresh 12-month low (3.60%), three straight declines, Sahm gap negligible — this is a clear tightening signal, not the "flat" I discounted before. My prior labor conviction (-0.05, essentially no view) was too timid; the multi-horizon data (3m/6m/12m all negative) now supports a real, if not extreme, tightening call. Inflation: headline CPI reaccelerated (8.54% y/y, 0.95% MoM, 9.91% 3m annualized) — this is the "core reacceleration above prior highs" scenario I flagged would reinforce my lean, except it's headline doing the reaccelerating while core PCE is merely plateauing (5.35%, roughly flat 3m change). That's a genuine headline/core divergence, but for near-term Fed reaction-function purposes, a hot and accelerating headline print combined with the labor market at cycle-low unemployment is unambiguously supportive of the Fed staying on a hiking path rather than pausing — the opposite of my prior -0.15 inflation conviction, which was wrong.

So the panel has moved against my prior read, not confirmed it. Last meeting I was leaning toward pricing less tightening (short DGS3MO, long DGS2, structured as a mild bull-steepener/duration long within the front end). That thesis is now undercut: tight labor + hot headline inflation argues for MORE near-term tightening pricing, meaning front-end yields should stay firm/rise further, and the curve within 2y should not be betting on early cuts. Financial conditions (tightening, NFCI at 52w high) and balance sheet (confirmed active runoff) both reinforce a more-restrictive environment but are already well-flagged, slower-moving, and largely priced — they don't drive the flip, they just corroborate it. I'm reversing the trade: move to a flatter/higher front-end level position rather than a steepener favoring the 2y over bills, since near-term hikes should keep short bills bid up rather than let the front end price cuts.

discounted.why: Slow, monotonic, already well-anticipated tightening trend — confirms but doesn't move near-term Fed pricing.

arbitrated.notes:
Labor and inflation remain the two drivers that actually move the front end, and both are largely unchanged in direction from last meeting but with important nuance. Labor: unemployment printed 3.60% again, flat at the cycle low, confirming continued tightness but with decelerating momentum and conviction only 0.30 (down from 0.60 prior read) — the labor analyst itself is now much less confident in further tightening, essentially flagging a floor. Inflation: headline CPI reaccelerated (8.98% y/y, 1.26% MoM), well above the analyst's own falsifier, and conviction is still 0.55 — this is a live, un-stale read confirming persistent inflation pressure, even though core PCE is softening. The inflation_expectations analyst (breakevens) is not my seat's core input (it's a market-based proxy, not the reaction-function driver I was told to weight), but its sharp decline is worth noting as a modest counter-signal on forward inflation persistence — I discount it since headline CPI is the more direct and current reaction-function input for near-term Fed pricing.

Net: the panel still supports pricing more near-term tightening, but the case is weaker than last meeting. Labor's tightening signal has lost conviction (deceleration, floor-like behavior near 3.60%), while inflation's hot headline print keeps the Fed's near-term reaction function leaning hawkish. Financial conditions tightening (NFCI at 52w high) and balance sheet noise are constraints, not primary drivers, and don't change the reaction-function call — FCI tightening is already a consequence of expected policy, not a new causal input. I hold the existing long-front-end-yields position but trim conviction slightly given labor's softened conviction and the breakeven decline, which introduces a genuine (if secondary) disinflationary signal not present last meeting.

discounted.why: Tightening NFCI is a consequence of policy expectations, not an independent near-term reaction-function driver.

arbitrated.notes:
Since last meeting, the two drivers that actually move the front end have both turned against my prior "more tightening" stance. Inflation (headline CPI) delivered the exact falsifier I had set: MoM at -0.01%, well below 0.4%, with a decelerating 3m core trend — this is a genuine reversal of my prior +0.55 "up" call, not noise, since it's a live, fast-moving read that reprices near-term Fed expectations. Labor tightness, meanwhile, is not the falsifier I specified (no print above 3.70%) but shows deceleration: 3m/6m changes shrinking sharply versus the 12m pace, sitting at a cycle-low plateau. That's consistent with a labor market that has stopped actively tightening, reinforcing rather than fighting the inflation signal — no more "labor loosening vs inflation hot" tension; both now point the same direction (less near-term hawkish pressure). Financial conditions and balance sheet are the constraint variables per mandate: financial conditions have just started easing over three prints, but off a range near the top (still fairly tight in absolute level) — I read this as a lagging confirmation rather than a lead. Balance sheet continuing to shrink is a slow-moving structural upward pressure on yields but not something that repositions near-term policy pricing month to month; I keep it as background, near-zero net weight on the front-end call.

Net: my previous read (more tightening priced in) has been meaningfully undercut by a real inflation reversal plus a stabilizing labor market. I'm flipping the front-end tilt toward pricing less near-term tightening — trimming the long-DGS2/long-DGS3MO tightening bet down, keeping only a small residual position rather than fully reversing, since financial conditions' loosening is only 3 data points and could snap back.

trade.rationale: Inflation reversal and labor stabilization argue for less near-term tightening priced; trim prior long-tightening stance, tilt toward a modest front-end richening favoring DGS2 over bills.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.50
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "down" call is validated: headline CPI y/y fell from 8.98% to 8.46% to 8.22%, and the last two MoM prints were -0.01% and 0.06% — a sharp deceleration from the 0.95-1.26% pace two months prior. Headline 3m annualized (5.32%) is now below the trailing y/y, and headline_yoy_change_3m is -0.32pp, both confirming cooling momentum, likely driven by energy price relief given the gap between headline and core. Core PCE y/y also ticked down to 5.11% from 5.32%, with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.24pp, though core remains far above target (+3.11pp) and the deceleration there is much more modest than in headline — core_3m_annualized at 4.75% is still uncomfortably high and only slightly below the y/y rate. So underlying persistence hasn't broken, but the immediate one-month trend in headline MoM (two consecutive near-zero prints) suggests the next headline y/y print is more likely to fall or stay roughly flat than to rebound. Policy language changes are cosmetic (invasion→war, added "food") and don't signal a change in the Fed's inflation assessment. Given two months of very soft MoM prints, I lean toward continued softening in headline y/y, but with weaker conviction than last time since a base-effect rebound is possible if energy reverses.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print rebounding above ~0.6-0.7% (e.g., from energy price reversal) or core PCE y/y reaccelerating above 5.3% would flip this call.

The level remains well below the ~4% natural rate (distance -0.40pp), and the Sahm-rule gap is trivial at +0.07pp, far from the ~0.5pp threshold that signals recession-onset loosening. The 3m change is exactly 0.00pp and 6m change is still negative (-0.30pp), meaning the broader trend over the past six months is still tightening, not loosening, despite this single uptick. Range position is very low (0.08 of the 12-month range), confirming the labor market sits near its tightest point in the observed window. This one-month uptick could be noise around a still-tight labor market rather than a turn. Job gains are described as "robust" and unemployment "remained low" in policy language, reinforcing that officials see no meaningful loosening yet. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up for the next print, since 3.60% has already recurred twice, but the overall trend and low level argue against calling this a genuine loosening signal yet.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm a turning point toward loosening; a print at or below 3.50% would suggest tightening is still active.

arbitrated.notes: Front-end call rests almost entirely on labor and inflation; balance sheet and financial conditions are constraints, not drivers of near-term repricing. Labor: unemployment ticked up to 3.60% but this repeats a prior print, 6m trend still shows tightening (-0.30pp), Sahm gap trivial, range position near tightest of the year. This is flat-to-marginally-loosening at most — not the clean loosening signal that would justify pricing out tightening aggressively. I keep this near flat, slightly softer than "no view" given the repeated uptick, but well short of a genuine turn. Inflation: headline CPI continues to decelerate sharply (two near-zero MoM prints, yoy falling from 8.98%→8.22%), and breakevens have broken down to fresh lows (2.48, lower-third of range, 63d change -0.17pp) — corroborating rather than contradicting the CPI analyst. Core PCE deceleration is real but modest (5.32%→5.11%), so underlying persistence hasn't broken, but the reaction function trades off the visible near-term print, and both inflation-adjacent readings now agree on "down." That corroboration strengthens conviction versus last meeting, where I had a single inflation read. Financial conditions are genuinely flat/noisy (conflicting 4w/13w) and balance sheet is a slow, well-anticipated runoff continuation — I keep both as background constraints only, not movers. Net: inflation cooling continues to dominate, labor is not confirming a hard loosening signal but isn't hot either. This slightly reinforces last meeting's less-tightening call, primarily via inflation corroboration (CPI + breakevens agreeing), while labor keeps me from adding much size. I hold the existing modest front-end position rather than resize.

arbitrated.notes: Labor and inflation are the two drivers that actually set the near-term path, and both came in essentially unchanged from last meeting rather than confirming my prior tightening lean. Labor is flat at 3.60% for three straight prints — genuinely stable, not loosening (my -0.15 last time was premature) and not re-tightening either. Inflation continues to decelerate on headline (7.12% y/y, new 12m low) but core PCE remains sticky at 5.46%, and the analyst explicitly flags that much of the headline improvement is base-effects, not fresh disinflation. That's weaker evidence for "more tightening" than my prior +0.35 conviction implied — the call is still down/decelerating but the persistence in core caps how much this should move the front end hawkish. Financial conditions have now confirmed a genuine easing reversal (NFCI back below -0.15, four-week momentum negative), which cuts against added near-term tightening being needed, though I treat this as a slower-moving constraint rather than a repricing trigger. Balance sheet continues its clean runoff, mechanical and already well-priced. Net: the reaction-function inputs are softer than last meeting — labor stable-not-loosening, inflation decelerating-but-sticky-core, easier financial conditions — arguing for trimming, not adding to, near-term tightening pricing. This doesn't flip the whole book to pricing cuts, since core inflation isn't broken, but it does erode the case for extra front-end yield staying priced in. I'm reducing conviction on my inflation-driven belly-cheapening trade and moving toward a more neutral-to-slightly-lower front-end stance, keeping a small position given the panel didn't show a clean turn, just a fade of the prior tightening thesis.

arbitrated.notes: Front-end reaction-function picture is essentially unchanged from last meeting, with one modest refinement. Labor tightness remains flat: unemployment at 3.50%, unchanged 3m, still 0.50pp below assumed natural rate, no Sahm signal — a tight but non-accelerating labor market that gives the Fed no fresh hawkish or dovish trigger. Inflation continues to cool on headline (6.40% y/y, soft MoM prints, decelerating 3m annualized), which was the core support for my prior "less tightening" lean. However, core PCE remains sticky at 5.21% y/y with 4.46% 3m annualized, and — importantly — inflation expectations (breakevens) are showing a genuine, if modest, upward drift off their lows, testing the top of the recent range. That is a live wrinkle: it tempers how much disinflationary conviction I should carry into front-end pricing, even though the analyst's own call is still "down" on headline for next print. Financial conditions and balance sheet continue on their prior trajectories (looser financial conditions, continued QT) — both slow-moving and already largely priced, so I treat them as background constraints rather than repricing catalysts, consistent with mandate. Net: the reaction function still points toward no more near-term hikes and a mild disinflationary lean, but the breakeven uptick argues against adding to a "less tightening" bet — it argues for holding, not extending. This validates keeping the existing modest position rather than resizing it in either direction. No driver has moved decisively enough (unemployment still range-bound, headline CPI cooling but core sticky, breakevens only mildly firmer) to justify overriding last meeting's read.

discounted.why: Slow-moving, already priced; not a near-term repricing catalyst per mandate.
Constraint, not reaction-function driver; long-run trend already anticipated by market.

arbitrated.falsifier: A core PCE print back above 5.6%, unemployment at/below 3.40%, or breakevens sustaining above 2.40% would revive near-term tightening risk; unemployment above 3.70% would flip toward pricing cuts.

risks.text: Sticky core inflation plus firming breakevens could force the Fed to hold longer than front end prices.
Small position size means little P&L risk, but also little conviction expressed if reaction function shifts fast.
Curve near 2y is flat-to-inverted; carry on the trade is thin, limiting cost of being wrong.

trade.rationale: Labor flat and inflation still cooling on headline support a mild less-tightening lean, but firming breakevens and sticky core caution against sizing up. Hold existing modest front-end flattener-lite tilt unchanged.

why.labor_tightness: Unemployment flat 3.50%, no 3m/6m trend break; no basis for a directional near-term policy signal.
why.inflation: Headline still cooling (6.40% y/y, weak MoM) but core PCE sticky at 5.21%, so softened conviction vs last meeting's -0.30.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI keeps grinding lower, confirming prior easing call; treated as slow/priced background constraint.
why.balance_sheet: QT continues unabated, no pause signal; slow-moving, already priced into front end.

  The position you are carrying: DGS2 +0.05, DGS3MO -0.05 (conviction 0.25).

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.75
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: fed_assets fell every week in the series, from 8,489,039 to 8,470,557, continuing a steady runoff. The 4-week change (-80,612) and 13-week change (-252,533) both confirm an ongoing, fairly consistent pace of balance-sheet reduction, with assets now 494,930 below the 52-week high and well under the 13w average (8,572,667.85), consistent with sustained QT rather than a plateau. The policy language explicitly reaffirms continued runoff of Treasury and agency MBS holdings per the announced plan, with no mention of tapering the cap or pausing reinvestment. There is no evidence of deceleration in the weekly decrements — the last several weekly drops (508,587→489,039→470,557) show pace roughly $18-19B/week, consistent with prior weeks. Nothing here suggests a reversal or plateau is imminent for next month-end. Absent any statement change signaling a slowdown or halt, the mechanical, policy-conforming runoff should continue at a similar pace into the next observation month-end. I maintain a confident "down" call, slightly below previous conviction only because the pace seems slightly more volatile week-to-week than perfectly linear, but directionally unambiguous.
Would change its mind if: Weekly fed_assets rising above the 13w average for multiple consecutive weeks, or statement language pausing/ending balance-sheet runoff.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high, fed_assets

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call at low conviction (0.25) was roughly right in that no clean break occurred, but the evidence actually leaned more flattening than I credited. The last print (-0.69) sits right at the low end of recent range, essentially matching the series minimum seen (-0.70) a couple readings back, and well below the 12-month high of +0.63. slope_range_position of 0.10 confirms the spread is near the bottom decile of its 12-month range. Momentum signals are consistently negative: -0.18pp over 21 days, -0.35pp over 63 days, and a steep -1.41pp over 252 days — all pointing toward further inversion/flattening, not stabilization. The last ten observations oscillate in a narrow band (-0.65 to -0.70) with no clear reversal, but the drift within that band is toward the more negative end (ending at -0.69 after touching -0.65). Policy language shows another rate hike (to 4-1/4–4-1/2%) with continued "ongoing increases" language, which historically pressures front-end yields up relative to long end, consistent with the flattening pressure already evident in dgs2 (4.21%) exceeding dgs10 (3.52%) by the full inverted spread. Given the continued restrictive tilt and multi-horizon negative momentum, I lean toward modest further flattening rather than flat, though the band-bound behavior limits conviction.
Would change its mind if: A move above -0.55pp with slope_change_21d turning positive would signal steepening; a break below -0.80 would confirm accelerating inversion.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, dgs2, dgs10

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues to decline steadily, from -0.12 to -0.29 over the 13-week window, with the most recent print (-0.29) the lowest in the series. The 4-week change (-0.08) and 13-week change (-0.18) are both firmly negative, and momentum shows no sign of deceleration — each successive weekly reading has been lower than the last for the entire visible history. This confirms my prior "down" call: conditions have continued to ease, not stalled or reversed. The index sits well below zero (-0.29), meaning financial conditions are looser than the historical average, and it is also below its own 13-week average (-0.20), reinforcing that the easing trend is still active rather than mean-reverting. The series sits at 0.57 of its 52-week range, roughly mid-range, still with room to fall toward the 52-week low of -0.54, and no sign of exhaustion in the trend. There is no indication in the measurements of a base or reversal forming. Absent a change in the pace of decline, the most reasonable expectation for month-end is a continuation of this easing trajectory, likely landing modestly below -0.29.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above -0.24 with 4-week change turning positive would signal the easing move has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_13w_avg, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (up, 0.35) was wrong: after peaking at 2.33 the breakeven has now printed two consecutive declines to 2.29 then 2.24, exactly the falsifier condition I set. The 10-year breakeven has round-tripped from 2.33 down to 2.24, a -0.09pp pullback in the last two observations, and the 21d and 63d changes are both negative (-0.03 and -0.21 pp respectively), confirming the recent momentum has turned down rather than resuming the earlier uptrend from 2.12. The series is still up considerably from its 12-month low (be_above_12m_low +0.12pp) and sits only 13% into its 12-month range, but the near-term trajectory is now clearly softening. Distance from the 2% target remains material at +0.24pp, so expectations are still anchored above objective, but the direction of travel over the last several readings is down, not up. Given two consecutive down-prints and negative short/medium-term change measures, I read this as a modest downward lean for the next month rather than a continuation of new highs. Conviction is moderate, not high, since the longer 252d change is smaller in magnitude and the level remains elevated relative to the 12-month low.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 2.29-2.33 over the next few observations would indicate the pullback was noise, not a new downtrend.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was directionally correct: headline y/y fell from 7.12 to 6.33 (-0.79pp), continuing the deceleration trend, and my stated falsifiers (MoM above 0.6% or core PCE back above 5.5%) did not trigger — outgoing MoM was 0.53% and core PCE fell to 4.97%, its lowest in the series. That said, the outgoing MoM of 0.53% is a step up from the prior month's 0.02%, and the 3m annualized rates (headline 3.28%, core 4.08%) show core running hotter than the y/y trend suggests, with core_yoy_change_3m only -0.64pp versus -1.43pp for headline. This gap (core_minus_headline -1.36pp) means headline's recent favorable readings are more energy/base-effect driven, while core PCE, though improving, remains far above target (+2.97pp) and its momentum is less convincingly decelerating. The yoy_range_position at 0.00 confirms headline is at its 12-month low, which is constructive, but the single-month MoM uptick warrants caution against expecting another sharp drop next month. Net: disinflation trend intact but momentum has softened compared to the pace of decline seen in prior months, so I expect continued but slower improvement.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print above 0.6% or core PCE y/y rising back above 5.2% would signal stalling/reacceleration and invalidate continued deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, core_minus_headline, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.60
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate has been essentially flat at cycle lows, printing 3.50% in the most recent observation, matching the trailing 12-month low and sitting well below the ~4% natural rate estimate (distance_from_natural -0.50pp). My prior flat call was correct: the series has oscillated narrowly between 3.50-3.60% for the last six readings with no directional break, and the latest print confirms that range-bound behavior rather than the loosening or renewed-tightening thresholds I set. The 3m change (-0.10pp) and 6m change (0.00pp) both indicate no meaningful momentum in either direction, and the Sahm gap remains negligible at +0.03pp, far from the ~0.5pp threshold that would signal recessionary loosening. Policy language continues to describe the labor market as robust with low unemployment, consistent with the data. With the rate pinned at its 12-month low and no acceleration in either direction visible in the last several prints, the most defensible call remains flat. There is a slight asymmetry — the level is very tight, and historically such tightness eventually gives way to gradual normalization — but nothing in the recent trend supports calling a turn yet. I maintain flat with slightly higher conviction than before given the confirmed stability.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm loosening; a print at or below 3.40% would suggest renewed tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "up" call at 0.45 conviction was wrong for this window: the 10y has actually drifted down over the last 21d (-0.31pp) and 63d (-0.44pp), even as the level sits at 3.52%, near the recent range but well off the extreme high. The fall satisfies part of my own falsifier (negative 21d momentum), though the level is still above 3.75 was never breached and 30s10s hasn't flattened (it's actually a modestly positive 0.13pp, little changed). Looking at the raw series, yields rose from 3.37 to a peak near 3.55 and have since chopped sideways/down slightly, ending at 3.52 — essentially a plateau with mild softening. The 252d change (+1.67pp) shows the dominant multi-quarter trend remains firmly up, and range position (0.71) confirms we're in the upper part of the 12-month band, consistent with continued balance-sheet runoff pressure on term premium. But the most recent short-term momentum is flat-to-slightly-down, not a continuation of the uptrend. Given the conflicting signals — strong medium-term uptrend vs. recent stalling — I'll moderate toward a flat-to-slightly-up call rather than reasserting a confident up view.
Would change its mind if: A decisive break below 3.40 with negative 21d momentum persisting and a flattening/inverting 30s10s slope would confirm the uptrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  curve_slope lacks financial_conditions — Tightening/loosening in broader conditions often co-moves with curve flattening/steepening pressure.
  curve_slope lacks term_premium — Rising term premium could independently steepen the curve regardless of Fed path.
  inflation_expectations lacks inflation — Realized CPI surprises often drive short-term breakeven swings and would clarify if this dip is durable.
  inflation_expectations lacks financial_conditions — Tightening financial conditions can compress breakevens independent of inflation views, would help disentangle cause.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of ongoing Treasury runoff/issuance supply would clarify whether upward pressure on long end is intensifying or easing.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment shifts could signal flight-to-quality flows that would push yields down independent of supply factors.

arbitrated.notes: Labor and inflation remain the only two drivers I'll price for the front end, and both still argue for less near-term tightening risk, but neither has flipped hard enough to justify resizing the existing small position. Labor: unemployment pinned at 3.50%, range-bound 3.50-3.60% for six prints, Sahm gap negligible — flat, no tightening impulse, but also no loosening signal that would pull forward cuts. This is a "no view" driver in direction terms; I keep conviction at 0 on labor_tightness itself (the rate isn't moving) while noting the level still means the Fed has no cover to ease on labor grounds alone. Inflation: headline CPI decelerating (7.12%→6.33%) and core PCE at cycle low 4.97%, but the MoM tick-up (0.53% vs 0.02% prior) and the elevated 3m-annualized core (4.08%) versus headline show disinflation losing speed, not reversing. That's consistent with my prior -0.35 call; I nudge it slightly less negative (-0.30) to reflect the softening momentum without abandoning the disinflation thesis. The inflation_expectations analyst (breakevens rolling over from 2.33 to 2.24) supports the same read but is a secondary confirmation, not a distinct reaction-function input — I fold it into inflation conviction rather than treat separately, since it lacks a direct read on realized CPI and could be financial-conditions-driven rather than inflation-driven. Financial conditions (NFCI easing) and balance sheet (continued QT) are the priced-in, slow-moving constraints — directionally unchanged from last meeting, still easing/still shrinking, but I treat them as background, not repricing catalysts for 2y-and-in. Net: modest disinflation continuing, labor still tight-but-stable — the front end should keep leaning toward slightly less tightening priced, unchanged from last month, so I hold the existing small curve-cheapening tilt rather than press it.

arbitrated.notes: Labor tightness remains the anchor for the front end and it is unchanged: unemployment at 3.6%, flat 3m/6m, Sahm gap negligible, still ~0.4pp below natural rate estimate. This is a genuine "flat" read with reasonably fresh data (20 days) and decent conviction — no basis to move off no-view on labor, and no loosening to offset inflation. Inflation (formed 13 days ago) continues to soften: headline CPI down to 5.96% y/y, core PCE at a 13-month low 4.94%, though the pace of core deceleration is slowing. This is consistent with my prior inflation call but slightly less convicted disinflation than before given decelerating core momentum. The wildcard is breakevens: inflation_expectations flipped from down to up, with 10y breakeven making a fresh series high (2.43) and a clear 1-3 month uptrend, though it's not yet through my stated falsifier of 2.30% sustained (it's now well above that, actually triggering it). That's a genuine change since last meeting and it argues for less confidence in the disinflation trade than I had. However, breakevens are a market-implied read, not the Fed's direct reaction-function input (CPI/PCE and labor are), and the 12m trend in breakevens is still net negative — so I treat this as a tempering signal, not a reversal driver. arbitrated.notes:
Labor and inflation remain the two drivers that actually move the front end, and both point the same direction as last meeting, reinforcing rather than reversing the prior call. Labor is flat — unemployment pinned at 3.50%, no momentum either way, a genuinely stable read with decent conviction but low information content since it's already fully priced (Fed reaction function needs a break above 3.70% or below 3.40% to move). Inflation continues to decelerate: headline CPI y/y fell a full point, core PCE grinding to a 12-month low, and inflation_expectations (breakevens) rolled over on 21d momentum after a brief bounce — corroborating rather than contradicting the CPI read. This is a genuine reinforcement of the disinflation call, not a stale repeat: the breakeven analyst explicitly reversed its own prior "up" lean, closing a gap that existed last meeting. Together, labor-flat + inflation-cooling continues to argue for less near-term tightening priced, consistent with my prior stance.

Financial conditions tightening (NFCI up, clear monotonic move) and the balance-sheet reversal (unwind of runoff, sharp asset growth) are the constraint layer, not the driver. The balance-sheet surge is a single-month spike with an admitted stale "why" and the analyst's own falsifier (unwind) is plausible — it doesn't override the reaction-function read, and per mandate, balance-sheet effects on yields are inverted (more assets = easier conditions = modest downward pressure on front-end pricing of hikes), which if anything reinforces the "less tightening" lean marginally, though I keep conviction low since it's a noisy one-month print.

No driver has moved decisively enough to flip the position. I hold the existing modest steepener-ish long-front tilt (long 2y, short 3mo) reflecting a slightly reduced pricing of imminent hikes without a strong directional call.

arbitrated.notes:
Labor tightness is unambiguously the dominant near-term driver here and it has NOT loosened — unemployment at 3.40% is a 12-month low, all trend windows negative, Sahm-gap trivial. That's a hawkish-leaning input relative to my prior "no view" (0.00), and it directly cuts against the disinflation story. Inflation itself is the second live driver and it just flipped: the analyst moved from "down" to "flat," citing exhausted base effects, sticky core PCE (4.78% y/y, 2.78pp above target), and momentum (core_3m_annualized 4.74%) that is no longer decelerating. That's a meaningful change from last meeting's -0.45 inflation call, which was the anchor for my prior less-tightening / bull-front-end position. Financial conditions and balance sheet are secondary, slow-moving inputs per my mandate — financial conditions is essentially flat/noisy (two-print pause within a tightening regime, not a reversal), and balance sheet's shift to "down" is a technical unwind of a repo-driven spike, not a change in the announced QT pace, so its policy-relevant signal is unchanged from last meeting.

Net: the two reaction-function drivers that actually move the front end are pointing toward LESS near-term disinflation than I had priced — labor stays tight, inflation momentum stalling rather than continuing to fall. This undercuts the basis for my prior small bull-front-end tilt (long DGS2 vs short 3mo). I should flip toward pricing slightly more near-term tightening risk, favoring a modest front-end flattener within 2y (higher short rates relative to 2y, or simply less duration-received bias). Given conviction is moderate (labor 0.55, inflation 0.40) and this is a shift not a reversal in kind, I'll resize modestly rather than go large.

Labor still flat, inflation still sticky at the core level despite headline noise, so I maintain a mild bias toward the front end continuing to price a bit more near-term tightening / less easing than markets might otherwise assume. Keeping my existing modest position, not resizing on a single ambiguous CPI print.

arbitrated.notes: The two reaction-function drivers that matter most for the front end — labor and inflation — both came back essentially flat, consistent with last meeting, and neither confirms the disinflation/loosening falsifier I set previously. Labor held at 3.60% (top of range, Sahm gap only 0.13pp, well below the 0.50pp turning-point threshold), so no loosening signal yet — still a tight labor market that keeps the Fed's reaction function neutral-to-hawkish rather than dovish. Inflation's headline y/y drop from 4.13 to 3.07 looks dramatic but the analyst is explicit it's base-effect driven, not a genuine deceleration: m/m at 0.22% is right in the recent average, core running hotter than headline on a 3m-annualized basis, and base effects are now exhausted so the mechanical tailwind fades. Neither driver crosses my prior flip thresholds (CPI m/m <0.05%, unemployment >3.60%), so I don't have a fresh reason to move off my existing framework. Financial conditions continue to ease but momentum is decelerating and near-zero — this is priced/slow and mostly a constraint, not a trigger. Balance-sheet runoff continues as expected, unchanged pace and language; this is well-anticipated and structurally supports higher term premium/duration supply, but it's a beyond-2y-relevant, slow-moving variable, not a near-term policy repricer. Nothing here argues for pricing more or less near-term tightening than last time — labor and inflation are both holding at "flat," which is itself informative: it removes the case for easing bias in Fed pricing but also doesn't support hawkish repricing. I hold my existing modest position rather than resize on a single data cycle that didn't confirm either falsifier.
