Core continues to decelerate only slowly (-0.40pp over 3m) and remains the better read on persistence - it's still elevated and sticky, arguing against a benign path for headline. Given the recent yoy uptick, the fading of favorable base effects, and core stickiness, I lean toward another slight increase in headline yoy at the next release, though momentum data alone (mom 0.20%, 3m avg 0.19%) doesn't support a sharp move.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.15% would confirm base effects aren't reaccelerating; above 0.35% would confirm a stronger uptrend.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, headline_3m_annualized, core_minus_headline

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print (3.50%) sits right in the middle of the 3.40-3.60% range that has held for 13 months, confirming my prior flat call was correct — the rate did not exceed 3.60% nor did it stay at the 3.40% low. The 12-month change is exactly 0.00pp, and the 6-month change is also 0.00pp, indicating no trend in either direction. The 3-month change of +0.10pp is trivial noise within a 0.20pp range and the Sahm-gap of 0.17pp is far below the 0.50pp threshold that would signal genuine loosening. The series has oscillated between 3.40 and 3.60 with no directional persistence — essentially a flat, tight labor market holding roughly 0.50pp below the natural rate. Policy language reinforces this: "job gains have been robust... unemployment rate has remained low," with only a forward-looking mention of credit tightening as a risk, not evidence of current loosening. Absent a break outside the established range, the base case for the next report is continuation of this tight, rangebound pattern rather than a directional move. I maintain a flat call, keeping conviction moderate given the small but real risk that credit tightening eventually shows up in hiring data.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.60% (new 12-month high) would confirm loosening; a drop to 3.40% or below would suggest renewed tightening beyond noise.
Leaned on: unrate_change_12m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_3m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_width, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y sits at 3.97, near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.83, 1.37pp above the 12m low of 2.60%). Momentum remains positive across all horizons: +0.12pp over 21d, +0.53pp over 63d, and a sizable +1.19pp over 252d, confirming a sustained uptrend rather than a one-off spike. The last print (3.97) ticked down slightly from 4.01 but stayed well above the prior cluster around 3.85-3.91, so this looks like a pause/consolidation within the uptrend rather than a reversal. My prior falsifier (a drop below 3.72 with negative momentum, or 30s10s flattening toward zero) has not been triggered — 30s10s is still modestly positive at +0.05pp, essentially unchanged from before, not flattening or inverting. Continued balance-sheet runoff (ongoing QT) remains a structural tailwind for long-end supply/term premium, consistent with the policy language shown. My previous "up" call was correct — yields continued to climb through this window. Given continued near-cycle-high levels, intact upward momentum, and no reversal signal in the slope or level, I maintain a modestly bullish (rising) call for the long-end term premium into next month-end, though the deceleration in 21d momentum warrants tempering conviction slightly.
Would change its mind if: A drop below 3.80 with negative 21d momentum, or 30s10s flattening toward zero/negative, would signal the uptrend has stalled.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.78
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The series continues its steady decline: total assets fell from 8,145,727 to 8,139,066 in the latest week, extending an unbroken downtrend across the 13-week window. The 4-week change (-104,278M) and 13-week change (-297,189M) both confirm continued runoff at a pace consistent with recent months, and the 52-week change (-712,370M) shows the cumulative QT effect remains intact with no sign of deceleration or plateau. Assets remain well below the 52-week high (-693,693M), and there is no evidence of reversal. My prior call (down, 0.78) was correct — the latest print did not flatten or reverse, it extended the decline. Policy language is unchanged, explicitly stating continued reduction of Treasury and agency holdings per previously announced plans, with no mention of tapering the runoff pace or pausing. Given the consistency of the weekly declines and the unchanged reinvestment-cap language, I expect the balance sheet to continue contracting into next month-end, likely by a similar magnitude to the recent 4-week pace. There is no data suggesting an inflection point is near.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print flat or rising, reversal above ~8.3T sustained, or policy language announcing a pause/end/taper of runoff.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (-0.76) sits within the recent choppy range and my prior "flat" call held up reasonably well: the spread bounced around -0.63 to -0.78 over the last ten readings without a decisive break. The most recent move is a slight re-inversion from -0.78 to -0.76, essentially noise. slope_change_21d is marginally positive (+0.04) while slope_change_63d is marginally negative (-0.04), a contradiction that signals no clear near-term trend — just chop within a well-worn band. The 252-day change (-0.46) confirms the larger backdrop has been one of flattening/inversion, but that move looks exhausted for now, with the curve sitting at 0.35 of its 12-month range (36% up from the most inverted point), not near either extreme. The FOMC hiked the funds rate this meeting (5.25-5.50% from 5.00-5.25%), which typically presses the front end up and can modestly flatten or re-invert the curve at the margin, consistent with the tiny recent dip. But this is a small, continued-tightening move already priced into the front end, not a regime shift. Absent a break outside the -0.85 to -0.60ish recent chop zone, I don't see conviction for a directional call either way.
Would change its mind if: A break above -0.60 (steepening) or below -0.90 (re-inversion) sustained for several sessions, with slope_change_63d confirming the same sign, would change my view.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series shows a steady, persistent decline: from -0.21 thirteen weeks ago to -0.32 now, with the 4-week change (-0.06) actually steeper than the 13-week change annualized pace, indicating easing is accelerating rather than stalling. The index sits at its 52-week low (nfci_above_52w_low = 0.00, range_position = 0.00), meaning conditions are the loosest they've been in the observed window. My prior "down" call was correct and the falsifier condition for confirming strengthening easing (continued decline below -0.28) has been met — the index is now well below that threshold at -0.32. There is no sign of reversal: every single week in the 13-observation series moved in the same direction (flat or lower), with no upticks. The distance from zero (-0.32) is also the largest in the series, meaning conditions are increasingly looser than historical average. Policy language remains unchanged, referencing cumulative tightening effects but not offering fresh signal on financial conditions directly. Given the consistency and acceleration of the decline, I expect continued easing into month-end, though I temper conviction slightly since 13-week average (-0.25) is still above current level, meaning any mean-reversion pause is possible after such a sustained run.
Would change its mind if: A reversal above -0.28 with positive 4-week change would signal tightening resuming; flattening near -0.32 for consecutive weeks would suggest stabilization.

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been invalidated: breakeven_10y has now dropped decisively below the 2.30 threshold I flagged as my reversal trigger, printing 2.24 at the latest observation. The recent path shows a clear downtrend: after peaking near 2.35 mid-series, breakevens have fallen for five of the last six readings, with be_change_21d at -0.12pp and be_change_252d at -0.34pp confirming both near-term and longer-term downward momentum. The 63d change is still slightly positive (+0.08pp), reflecting the earlier bounce, but that has been fully reversed and then some in the most recent weeks. be_range_position at 0.29 (of the 12-month 2.10-2.59 range) shows breakevens sitting in the lower third of their annual range, and the series is now only 0.14pp above the 12-month low. Distance from the 2% target has compressed to +0.24pp, the smallest gap in this window, indicating market-implied inflation expectations are anchoring closer to target rather than drifting higher. With no offsetting evidence of stabilization in the last two prints (2.26, 2.24), the trend looks intact rather than exhausted. I'm revising to a down call with moderate conviction, expecting continued drift toward or through the 2.20 area absent a reversal catalyst.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above ~2.30 over the next few prints would signal the downtrend has stalled and reversed.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  My prior call (up, 0.40) is validated: headline m/m came in at 0.48%, well above my 0.35% reacceleration threshold, and headline yoy rose from 3.29% to 3.72% — a second consecutive monthly increase off the 3.07% trough. The 12-month yoy_range_position of just 0.13 confirms we're near cycle lows, but the last two prints (3.29→3.72) show clear upward momentum in headline, driven by base effects rolling off and a hot m/m print. Core PCE continues to decelerate steadily (4.38→4.30), and core_3m_annualized (2.80%) is much closer to target than headline_3m_annualized (3.67%), so underlying persistent inflation is still cooling. But my mandate is to call headline_cpi_yoy specifically, and headline is clearly turning: two straight months of yoy acceleration plus a strong 0.48% m/m print (above the 3m average of 0.30%) signal that the disinflation trend has stalled and reversed at the margin. Policy language ("inflation remains elevated," "highly attentive to inflation risks") is consistent with this. I'll raise conviction modestly given confirmed reacceleration, though core's continued cooling tempers how far I lean.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.20% or a drop in yoy back toward 3.3-3.5% would signal the reacceleration was a one-off, not a trend.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, outgoing_mom

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 3.70%, a new 12-month high, up from 3.50% the prior month and 0.30pp above the trailing 12-month low of 3.40%. This is exactly the threshold I said would confirm loosening. The unrate_range_position sits at 1.00 (top of the 12-month range), and the sahm_gap (0.20pp) has widened. While the level itself (3.70%) is still only modestly above the ~4% natural rate estimate, so it isn't a crisis reading, the trajectory is now clearly upward rather than range-bound noise: three consecutive readings show 3.40 → 3.60 → 3.60 → 3.50 → 3.70, with the last move being the largest single-month jump in the series. My prior flat call was wrong in direction terms — this is no longer sideways chop, it's a break to a new high. One month alone doesn't confirm a Sahm-rule trigger (which needs a 0.50pp rise from the 12-month low sustained), but the change_3m and change_12m both ticking to +0.10pp while the single-month move was +0.20pp suggests acceleration. I'm calling this a loosening labor market, not yet severe, but a genuine shift, not a level call.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to 3.50% or below next month would suggest this was noise; a further rise toward 3.80%+ would confirm a genuine loosening trend.
Leaned on: unrate_12m_high, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_change_3m, unrate_above_12m_low

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year has drifted lower over the most recent observations (4.34→4.09), with the last four prints ticking down (4.20, 4.12, 4.12, 4.09). My prior "up" call is being contradicted by the latest data: 21-day change is now essentially flat (+0.01pp) versus the strong 63-day (+0.48) and 252-day (+0.97) gains that drove the earlier call. The uptrend has clearly lost momentum in the near term, even though the yield remains high in its 12-month range (0.80 range position, 0.98pp above the 12m low) and 30s10s (+0.11) is still modestly positive rather than inverted. So the longer-run backdrop (elevated level, positive term structure, ongoing balance-sheet runoff pressure) still argues for a higher-for-longer long end, but the immediate trajectory has flattened to slightly down. Given the mixed signal — strong medium/long-term uptrend context but fading near-term momentum — I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for the next month rather than continuing the "up" call. This is a partial correction of my prior view: the uptrend has stalled per my own falsifier condition on momentum, though not the 30s10s flattening trigger.
Would change its mind if: A renewed break above 4.25 with positive 21d momentum, or clear steepening in 30s10s, would revive the uptrend case.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: Labor and inflation are the two reaction-function drivers I weight most, and both moved this meeting in a way that partially offsets each other but nets toward slightly less near-term tightening than my prior read implied. Labor_tightness: unemployment jumped to a new 12-month high (3.70%, up from 3.50%), the largest single-month move in the series — this is a genuine loosening signal, not noise, and it's a fresh read (23 days) directly relevant to the front end. Inflation: headline CPI reaccelerated (yoy 3.29%→3.72%, m/m 0.48%), which argues the other way, but the analyst's own falsifier flags 0.20% m/m or a drop back toward 3.3-3.5% yoy as reversal signals — we're not there, so I keep some upward lean, but core PCE continues decelerating (4.38→4.30), tempering how much weight headline reacceleration should get for a Fed that has said it watches core/underlying trend. On balance, labor loosening is now the more decisive break (new high, largest jump) versus inflation which is still mixed between headline-up/core-down. Financial conditions (NFCI at 52-week low, easing accelerating) and balance sheet (still steadily contracting) are treated as background constraints, not reaction-function movers — both continue in the same direction as last meeting, so no update needed there, and neither should override the labor signal per mandate. Net: the labor print is the delta since last meeting and it points toward pricing marginally LESS near-term tightening than before, though not a full reversal since inflation hasn't confirmed disinflation. I trim my short front-end/tightening tilt but don't flip it outright — my previous falsifier (CPI <0.10% or unemployment >3.60%) has been partially met on the labor side (3.70% > 3.60% threshold), which is the actual trigger for a directional shift.

arbitrated.notes:
Labor and inflation—the two drivers that actually matter for near-term Fed pricing—both softened toward flat/no-view relative to last meeting. Labor_tightness moved from an "up" (loosening, -0.50 conviction on tightness falling) to a plateau read: unemployment held at 3.70% for two straight months, 3m change decelerating, Sahm gap still well below the 0.50 recession trigger. That's a genuine change in the evidence: the loosening thesis I carried is stalling, not accelerating. Inflation similarly cooled its "up" call: headline yoy actually ticked down (3.72→3.69), core PCE keeps decelerating steadily, and the analyst's own conviction dropped to 0.30 leaning only slightly up, driven mostly by base-effect arithmetic rather than fresh momentum. Neither driver clears my stated falsifier thresholds (unemployment toward 3.80%+, CPI m/m below 0.20%) but both are moving in the direction that would eventually get there — labor loosening has paused rather than reversed, and inflation has stalled rather than reaccelerated.

Financial conditions are flat/stabilizing (own analyst downgraded conviction to 0.35), and balance sheet continues its high-conviction, well-anticipated runoff — both are slow-moving, already-priced constraints, not near-term repricing catalysts, so I keep them as background context rather than drivers of the front-end call.

Net: the reaction-function evidence is weaker and more balanced than last meeting — less clear-cut disinflation-with-loosening-labor than I had, but nothing has flipped toward more tightening either. The right move is to trim the "less tightening" tilt rather than hold it at full size or reverse it. I'll keep the general shape (mild bull-steepening bias, price out a touch more near-term tightening) but shrink conviction given the stalling evidence on both live drivers.

discounted.why: Stabilizing/flat, slow-moving and largely priced; not a near-term repricing catalyst for front end.
High-conviction but slow, well-anticipated runoff already priced into front-end curve.

trade.rationale: Trim prior mild steepener toward flat given stalling (not reversing) labor loosening and inflation deceleration; keep small directional tilt pending clearer confirmation.

Labor: unemployment jumped to 3.90% with three consecutive rises (3.70/3.70/3.90) and the Sahm gap approaching turning-point territory \u2014 this is a genuine loosening signal, not noise, and directly hits my own falsifier for resuming the price-out-tightening trade. Inflation: headline CPI yoy dropped sharply (3.69\u21923.25), m/m came in at 0.14% (below my 0.15% flip threshold), core PCE continues a steady descent, and 3m annualized core is near target-consistent pace. Both drivers that matter most for near-term Fed pricing have now cleared their respective falsifier bars in the same direction: less tightening, more likely cuts get pulled forward or hikes stay off the table. This is a clear break from last meeting where labor was only mildly loosening (-0.15 conviction) and inflation leaned up (+0.15). arbitrated.notes: The two reaction-function drivers have both moved against my prior read, and they now point in the same direction rather than offsetting. Labor: unemployment jumped to 3.90% with three consecutive rises (3.70/3.70/3.90) and the Sahm gap approaching turning-point territory — this is a genuine loosening signal, not noise, and directly hits my own falsifier for resuming the price-out-tightening trade. Inflation: headline CPI yoy dropped sharply (3.69→3.25), m/m came in at 0.14% (below my 0.15% flip threshold), core PCE continues a steady descent, and 3m annualized core is near target-consistent pace. Both drivers that matter most for near-term Fed pricing have now cleared their respective falsifier bars in the same direction: less tightening, more likely cuts get pulled forward or hikes stay off the table. This is a clear break from last meeting where labor was only mildly loosening (-0.15 conviction) and inflation leaned up (+0.15). Financial conditions have firmed modestly (NFCI drifting toward -0.28) but remain historically loose in absolute terms — a real but secondary constraint, not enough to offset the labor/inflation combination. Balance sheet continues its steady, well-anticipated runoff — priced already, and per mandate treated as backdrop, not a near-term repricing driver. Given labor and inflation have both flipped through their stated triggers, I'm resizing off the prior thin, near-flat position toward a modest bull-steepening bias within the front end: richer at the short end (3mo/6mo more anchored by cuts-pricing) versus 2y, reflecting a genuine reduction in near-term tightening odds without taking a long-end view.

discounted.why: Real but secondary; still historically loose, doesn't override labor/inflation reaction-function signal.

trade.rationale: Labor loosening and resumed disinflation both clear their falsifier triggers, favoring less near-term tightening priced — richening the very front end (3mo) relative to 2y, a modest front-end bull-steepener within the 2y ceiling.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35

Report:
  Last time I called "down" on continued disinflation momentum, and the newest print vindicates that: headline yoy fell from 3.25 to 3.13, and the month/month print was 0.15%, below the 0.35% threshold that would have flagged renewed acceleration. Core PCE continues its steady grind lower (3.69 to 3.47 yoy), and the 3-month annualized core (2.31%) is now essentially at target, with headline 3m annualized also at 2.73%. Momentum indicators (headline_yoy_change_3m -0.59pp, core_yoy_change_3m -0.83pp) confirm the trend is still disinflationary, not stalling. However, headline yoy is now sitting near its 12-month low (yoy_above_12m_low only 0.06pp), which means further big yoy declines require continued soft base effects — the low-hanging fruit of easy comparisons may be diminishing, and outgoing_mom (0.02) being low means the base being dropped is easy to beat, which could make next month's yoy comparison marginally harder if new prints run at the 3m average pace (0.22%). Still, with mom prints modest and no signs of reacceleration in either headline or core, I lean toward continued flat-to-slightly-lower readings rather than a reversal upward. Conviction is moderate given the yoy is near cycle lows and base effects are less favorable going forward, tempering the disinflation call.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or yoy rising back above 3.4% would signal renewed acceleration rather than continued disinflation.

  The latest print pulled back to 3.70% from the 3.90% spike, exactly within the 3.60-3.70% range I flagged as the "noise" scenario for the prior 3.90 reading. This confirms my prior up call was too aggressive in conviction — the 3.90 print looks like a one-month outlier rather than the start of accelerating loosening. That said, the broader trend still shows some drift: the 3m avg (3.77%) sits above the 12m low (3.40%), the unrate is up 0.30pp from that low, and the range position (0.60) shows we're in the upper half of the past year's range. The Sahm gap of 0.37pp is still meaningfully below the 0.50 trigger threshold, so no recession-signal loosening is confirmed. Policy language changes are modest — "moderated since earlier in the year" versus "slowed in recent months" is essentially a rewording, not a signal of a labor-market shift, and both versions still describe the unemployment rate as "remained low." Given the pullback from 3.90 to 3.70, and the still-low absolute level (-0.30pp from natural rate), I see a modestly loosening bias remains from the 12-month trend, but with much reduced urgency after the retracement. This is a borderline call between flat and a mild up-lean.
Would change its mind if: A further rise back to 3.90%+ would revive the loosening signal; a drop to 3.50-3.60% would confirm the market remains genuinely tight.

Balance-sheet runoff continues per the unchanged policy language, which is a mild structural upward pressure on term premium, but it's being outweighed right now by the recent price action. Net: momentum has turned down after a multi-month climb, so I lean toward a flat-to-lower yield at month end rather than continuing the "up" call.

arbitrated.notes: Labor and inflation — the two reaction-function drivers I actually weight — both softened from the outlier readings that had briefly justified more hawkish pricing, but neither delivered a fresh directional signal versus last meeting. Labor: unemployment retraced from 3.90% back to 3.70%, squarely in the "noise" band the analyst itself flagged; Sahm gap still well below trigger. This is a confirmation of my prior loosening lean, not a new loosening impulse — conviction on labor should if anything come down slightly since urgency faded. Inflation: headline yoy fell again (3.25→3.13), mom soft at 0.15%, core PCE grinding lower — disinflation intact, though the analyst flags base effects turning less favorable, tempering the pace of further declines. Net: both drivers still point toward less near-term tightening being priced, consistent with my prior call, but the magnitude of conviction is lower than last time because (a) labor's move was a retracement of noise rather than confirmation of a new trend, and (b) inflation's easy comps are running out. Financial conditions and balance sheet remain the constraint, not the driver: FCI reversed back to easing (down), balance sheet continues firm runoff (down) — both roughly as before, mildly offsetting in their yield implications but I don't let them override the labor/inflation read per the mandate. No jobs report reversal to 3.60-3.70 hawkish surprise, no hot CPI print — the explicit falsifiers from last meeting did not fire. I hold the position but trim conviction slightly given the softer urgency in the labor signal and diminishing disinflation runway.

arbitrated.notes: The two reaction-function drivers are pulling apart further than last meeting, and the tension resolves toward LESS near-term tightening being priced, reinforcing rather than reversing the prior call. Labor: unemployment rose to 3.80% from a 3.40% trailing low, a 0.40pp climb sitting right at the Sahm threshold, with 3m/6m/12m changes all positive and range position at 0.80 (near the loose end). This is a clear, live loosening signal — labor tightness is falling, which argues for less near-term hiking pressure. Inflation: headline CPI yoy ticked up to 3.32% from 3.13%, a surprise against the prior "down" call, but core PCE continues decelerating (3.83→3.69→3.47→3.29, 3m annualized 2.31%) and the headline/core gap has closed. The report itself downgrades conviction to 0.30 flat given the surprise. I read this as inflation stabilizing near target on the trend measures the Fed actually weights (core), even though the single-month headline print was noisy. Neither falsifier from last meeting triggered cleanly — jobs report did NOT go back to 3.60% (it worsened to 3.80%, reinforcing my read), and CPI yoy is at 3.32%, below the 3.4% two-consecutive-print flip threshold. So the panel has not moved against me; if anything, labor loosening is now more decisive than a month ago. Financial conditions (NFCI at 52-week low, easing) and balance sheet (persistent QT runoff, hawkish structural offset) are the constraint layer: FC easing modestly supports risk-on/less need for tightening, while balance-sheet runoff is a slow, well-anticipated structural force with limited near-term repricing power. Net: labor loosening is now the dominant, live driver; inflation is roughly neutral-to-softening on core measures despite headline noise. I maintain the existing position, modestly reinforced by the labor read, rather than resizing on a single CPI print.

arbitrated.notes:
The two reaction-function inputs I weight most both softened relative to last meeting's read, but for different reasons. Labor: unemployment reversed from 3.90 back to 3.70%, landing right in the zone the analyst itself flagged as its falsifier for the prior "up" (loosening) call — the panel now reports "flat" with conviction cut to 0.20. That's a genuine signal against my prior -0.50 labor call: the loosening thesis lost its main support. Inflation: headline CPI continued to cool (3.09% y/y, near 12m lows), core PCE still descending, 3m annualized well below trend — the analyst's own call is "down" at 0.35, a bit softer than before but directionally the same as my prior +0.15 (I had that backwards in sign convention last time; disinflation continuing is consistent with the easing-tightening-fears thesis). Inflation expectations are pinned flat, not adding fresh disinflationary or reflationary information.

Net effect on the reaction function: labor's loosening signal has weakened materially (from a confident falsifier-triggering call to a noisy, low-conviction flat), while inflation continues to cool. The combination still argues for LESS near-term tightening priced, but the conviction behind that call should shrink because the labor leg — the more repricing-relevant of the two — is now much softer and closer to "no view." Financial conditions and balance sheet continue to ease/shrink respectively at a decelerating pace, consistent with prior, and I treat them as confirming background rather than drivers of a front-end repricing.

Given the labor read has moved from "confidently loosening" to "genuinely uncertain," I should reduce (not reverse) my dovish front-end position. The core thesis (less tightening priced) still holds directionally on inflation, but sizing should shrink given weaker labor conviction.

arbitrated.notes: Both reaction-function drivers have turned since last meeting, and both turned in the same direction: toward more near-term tightening priced, not less. Labor tightness flipped from my prior +0.05 (near-flat) to a confirmed uptrend — unemployment at 3.90%, a 12-month high, with 3/6/12m changes all positive and the analyst's own falsifier for reversion (3.70%) not met. That is a loosening labor market, which is the dovish read for the reaction function (higher unemployment = less tightening needed), so this driver argues for LESS near-term tightening, consistent with my prior stance. Inflation, however, moved the other way: my prior -0.35 (disinflation continuing) is now explicitly retracted by the analyst — headline CPI m/m printed 0.41%, above their own falsifier of 0.40%, with two consecutive accelerating m/m prints and headline_3m_annualized (3.71%) now running above trailing yoy. This is a live, first-hand reversal of the disinflation narrative, not noise-level chop. Given the mandate to resolve tension by asking which driver the reaction function weights now — with labor loosening gradually but not sharply (Sahm gap still below 0.50 threshold) while inflation shows a concrete, threshold-breaching acceleration — inflation is the more decision-relevant signal this month. It directly threatens my carried short-front-end-yield tilt. Financial conditions and balance sheet continue to ease/tighten as before (slow-moving, largely priced) and I treat them as background constraint, not signal. Net: my prior read (less tightening) is now under real pressure. I'll trim, not reverse, given labor's loosening still argues the other way and one hot CPI month is explicitly flagged by the inflation analyst as not yet a clean reacceleration story.

arbitrated.notes:
Labor and inflation remain the two drivers that actually move front-end pricing, and both continue to point in a modestly hawkish direction, consistent with last meeting. Inflation: headline CPI has now printed three consecutive strong months (0.31/0.41/0.44 m/m), 3m annualized at 4.75%, y/y up to 3.49% from 3.16% — a clear reacceleration that is fresh evidence, not stale. This is a genuine upgrade from last meeting's "leans higher" (0.45) toward a firmer "up" call. Core PCE continues decelerating, but headline is what drives near-term Fed rhetoric and market pricing of the next few meetings, so I weight headline over core here. Labor: unemployment held flat at 3.90% for two straight prints rather than continuing to rise — this is softer momentum than my prior read assumed, though the level is still at a 12-month high and the trend since last year is one of gradual loosening. This is a genuine but partial dilution of the labor-loosening case: not a reversal, just deceleration in the pace of loosening. Financial conditions have stalled near multi-week lows (first uptick in 13 weeks) — a slow, well-anticipated driver I discount per mandate. Balance sheet continues steady, unsurprising runoff — also a slow-moving, already-priced constraint, not a repricer of near-term policy.

Net: inflation's reacceleration is the dominant repricing driver this month, modestly reinforced by labor still being tight (even if plateauing rather than accelerating). This is not a big regime change from last meeting, but it leans slightly further toward "less near-term easing priced" than my prior stance, given inflation's upgrade outweighs labor's deceleration. I hold the existing modest short-front-end-cuts tilt, marginally sized toward the front (3mo) reflecting near-term repricing.

discounted.why: Stalling but slow-moving and largely anticipated; not a near-term policy repricer.
Steady, well-telegraphed runoff pace; constraint already priced, not a fresh signal.

arbitrated.falsifier: A headline CPI m/m print back below 0.20% or a jobs print below 3.70% would flip this back toward more near-term easing priced.

risks.text: Fed may weight decelerating core PCE over hot headline, discounting the inflation reacceleration story.
Front-end short is a modest, low-conviction position vulnerable to a single soft data surprise.
Holding a short 3mo position against flat/easing financial conditions carries limited carry benefit if Fed stays on hold.

trade.rationale: Inflation reacceleration outweighs labor's plateauing loosening, supporting modest less-easing-priced tilt: small long 2y / short front-3mo slope within 2y horizon.

why.labor_tightness: Unemployment held flat at 3.90% for two prints, not rising further; softer momentum than prior 'up' 0.50 call implied.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI easing has stalled with first uptick in 13 weeks; treated as slow/priced constraint, minimal near-term view.
why.balance_sheet: Steady, unsurprising QT pace continues; own-measurement falls, but treated as slow and already priced into curve.

There is no sign of deceleration — the last few prints (7,439,558 → 7,438,176 → 7,405,506 → 7,402,434) show the runoff continuing smoothly, with the latest weekly decline (~$3B) smaller than some prior weeks but still negative, not a reversal. My prior down call was correct: the four weeks since have shown continued declines, matching expectations. Given unchanged policy language and a consistent multi-week downtrend with no stabilization or reversal signal, I expect total assets to be lower again at next month-end, likely by a similar magnitude to recent 4-week changes (roughly $60-90B), absent any new policy announcement.

The most recent two prints (-0.34, -0.35) suggest the flattening move has resumed and is not yet reversing. With policy language unchanged (Fed on hold, data-dependent, no explicit signal), there's no fresh catalyst. Given the choppy, non-trending pattern within a fairly narrow band over the last few readings, I lean toward continued modest flattening but with low conviction given the whipsaw.

The index remains deeply negative (-0.41 vs zero average), meaning conditions are still much looser than historical average despite the marginal recent firming. My prior flat call was directionally fine — the index didn't break out of its range in either direction, though the slight uptick suggests conditions may be inching toward tighter (less loose) rather than looser. The move is too small and too recent to call a trend; it needs to persist or accelerate to be meaningful. Given the tiny 4-week change (+0.02) against a 13-week change of -0.02, the signal is genuinely mixed — a short-term uptick within a longer flat-to-slightly-easing window. I'll lean toward flat again, with a slight tilt toward up given the recent 3-observation uptick, but conviction remains modest.

However, the most recent three prints (2.42, 2.43, 2.41, 2.41) show deceleration/stalling rather than continued acceleration, suggesting the move higher may be pausing near cycle highs. Given the flattening in the last few observations after a longer uptrend, I lean toward continued near-term stability, but the underlying trend evidence still tilts modestly upward given the multi-month momentum. I'll call this flat-to-slightly-up, with low-moderate conviction, acknowledging the recent stall while respecting the medium-term uptrend.

Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI m/m came in at 0.22%, below the recent hot prints (0.31, 0.41, 0.44) and below my 0.20% flip threshold on a rounding basis but still slightly above it in substance. My prior "up" call was directionally weak — headline y/y actually ticked down from 3.49 to 3.36, so I was wrong to expect further acceleration; the sequence topped out and is now easing back. Core PCE y/y also ticked up marginally to 3.12 from 3.06, essentially flat and still hovering near 3.1%, not falling through 3.0% as my falsifier specified, but not accelerating either. The 3m annualized rates (headline 4.36%, core 4.78%) remain well above the 2% target and above the recent y/y readings, meaning underlying momentum is still running hot relative to the trailing year-over-year figure — this argues for some upward or sideways drift in coming prints rather than renewed disinflation. However, the single-month deceleration in headline m/m (0.22% vs 0.36% avg) and the pullback in headline y/y from its recent peak both point toward flat-to-slightly-down near-term. Net: momentum indicators are mixed — 3m annualized still elevated, but latest monthly print decelerating. I'll call this closer to flat, leaning down slightly, given the topping pattern in y/y and cooling in the latest month.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in headline m/m above 0.35% or core PCE y/y breaking above 3.3% would revive the acceleration case; sustained sub-0.20% prints would confirm disinflation.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

Call: flat, conviction 0.25

Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print held at 3.90%, unchanged from the prior two readings, so my "up" call from last time was not confirmed further — the rate did not push higher, it simply plateaued at the cycle high rather than falling back into the 3.70-3.80 range that would have signaled stabilization. The unemployment rate sits at the top of its 12-month range (range_position=1.00), 0.40pp above the 12-month low, with a Sahm gap of 0.40pp — close to but not yet crossing the classic 0.50 recession-signal threshold. The 3-month change is +0.20pp (mild upward drift) while the 6-month change is flat at 0.00pp, suggesting the rise has stalled rather than accelerated. Policy language actually turned slightly more upbeat, dropping the "moderated" qualifier and simply stating job gains "have remained strong" alongside a still-low unemployment rate — a modestly hawkish-on-labor shift in tone that argues against imminent further loosening. Level (3.90%) remains close to the natural rate (-0.10pp distance), not stretched. Given the plateau at the high end of the range with no fresh deterioration and firmer policy language, I lean toward flat, with a slight tilt up given proximity to the Sahm threshold and the recent 3m uptick.
Would change its mind if: A print at 4.00%+ would confirm renewed loosening; a drop back to 3.70-3.80% would confirm stabilization/reversal rather than plateau.

Momentum measures confirm this: +0.36pp over 21 days, +0.63pp over 63 days, and +1.16pp over 252 days — all positive and accelerating in the shorter windows, indicating sustained upward pressure rather than a one-off move. The yield sits at 0.82 of its 12-month range (position near the high end), well above the 12-month low (+1.32pp above it) and close to the 12-month high. The 30s10s slope is positive at +0.10pp, consistent with modest additional long-end premium building relative to the 10-year itself, not a flattening that would signal receding term premium. Continued balance-sheet runoff (still-active QT language) provides an ongoing structural tailwind for higher long-end yields via increased net supply absorption needs. My prior "flat" call was too cautious given this data was already trending up — the level and momentum evidence look more consistent with a genuine, if moderate, upward drift than noise. I'm revising to a modest up call.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 4.55% with negative 21d momentum and 30s10s flattening would reverse this call toward flat or down.

arbitrated.notes:
Both reaction-function drivers I actually trade off — labor and inflation — came in flat with soft conviction this month, and the marginal information in each cuts slightly against the "up" tilt I carried last meeting. Labor: unemployment held flat at 3.90% for a third month, a plateau at cycle highs rather than a fresh deterioration; the panel's own tone shifted marginally hawkish (dropped "moderated" language), so this is genuinely flat, not a loosening signal, but it is also not confirming the "up" (looser labor) call I had leaned on before. Inflation: headline CPI m/m actually decelerated to 0.22%, below my prior flip threshold in substance, and y/y ticked down from 3.49 to 3.36 — my prior "up" inflation call was wrong on direction. Core PCE y/y is flat near 3.1%, still hot in level but not accelerating. Net, the CPI print undercuts the case for pricing in more near-term tightening; my inflation conviction should come down and flip modestly negative rather than staying at +0.55.
Balance sheet continues its clean, un-decelerating runoff — high conviction, but this is a slow, well-telegraphed process the market has already largely priced; it matters more for term premium than for front-end policy pricing, so I keep it as background support rather than a lever for the 2y-and-in trade. Financial conditions are rangebound noise, no view worth trading.
Combining a stalled-not-falling labor market with a cooling CPI print, the reaction-function read now leans toward slightly less near-term tightening priced, a reversal of last meeting's modest hawkish tilt. I'm trimming the long-2y/short-3mo level position toward flatter/smaller, consistent with reduced conviction that the Fed needs to price more hikes near-term, but not flipping to an aggressive easing bet since labor didn't actually break down.

discounted.why: Slow, well-telegraphed QT already priced; matters more for term premium than front-end policy path.
Rangebound within noise, no directional signal for near-term policy pricing.

Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has been essentially flat and very stable for the last 13 weeks, drifting in an extremely narrow band (-0.44 to -0.41). The most recent readings have plateaued at -0.41 for five consecutive observations, with 4-week change at 0.00 and 13-week change at only +0.01. Conditions remain deeply looser than average (distance from zero of -0.41), sitting near the loose end of a 52-week range (range_position 0.12, close to the 52w low of -0.44 and far from the 52w high of -0.21). This is not a period of tightening or easing momentum — it's a stable, low-volatility plateau at accommodative levels. My prior flat call was correct: the index did not move meaningfully, staying pinned at -0.41. With no new policy language addressing this driver, and the trend showing no acceleration in either direction, the same conclusion holds today. There's no evidence of a shift toward the -0.36 tightening threshold or a fresh low below -0.44. The signal is unambiguous in its lack of movement, so I maintain a flat call, with slightly higher conviction given the extended plateau reinforces the stability read.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move to -0.36 or higher over 2+ weeks would confirm tightening; a fresh low below -0.44 would confirm renewed easing.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low, nfci_52w_high

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year breakeven has been essentially range-bound over the last 10 readings (2.30-2.36), with the most recent print at 2.35, down slightly from a local peak of 2.36. My prior "flat" call holds up reasonably well: neither the 2.45 upside break nor the 2.38 downside reversal threshold I flagged was hit — the series simply drifted within its recent band. The 21-day change is -0.03pp (mild pullback) while the 63-day change is +0.03pp and the 252-day change is +0.13pp, indicating a modest uptrend over the past year that has stalled into consolidation over the last month. Range position at 0.60 shows breakevens sitting in the upper-middle of the 12-month range, not stretched. Distance from the 2% target remains +0.35pp, a persistent but not widening gap — expectations are anchored somewhat above target without accelerating. There's no fresh evidence of a shift in market-implied inflation expectations; the small recent dip (2.36→2.35) is noise within a tight band, not a trend reversal. I see no catalyst in the policy language (unchanged boilerplate) to alter this. I'll maintain a flat call, perhaps with slightly more confidence given the continued tight range and absence of momentum in either direction.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 2.45 would confirm renewed upward momentum; a drop below 2.28 would signal a genuine downside reversal.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "flat" call was reasonably validated: headline CPI y/y ticked down to 3.24 from 3.36, and the outgoing m/m print of 0.05% was well below my 0.20% threshold, actually flagging renewed disinflation rather than acceleration. Core PCE y/y also continued its gradual descent to 3.01, within 1pp of target, extending a multi-month downtrend (core_yoy_change_3m -0.15pp). The very low 0.05% m/m headline print is the most striking new data point — it pulls the 3m annualized headline pace down toward 2.87%, back near target, though this could be noisy given the volatility in monthly prints (0.44, 0.22, 0.05 last three). Core 3m annualized at 3.64% still runs hotter than headline, indicating some underlying persistence, but the newest single readings (both headline and core) point down, not up. Policy language shift is notable — dropping "moving into better balance" for backward-looking "moved toward better balance over the past year" alongside a new line about "lack of further progress" suggests the Committee itself sees stalled progress, a mild hawkish tilt in rhetoric. But the hard data — especially the low outgoing m/m and continued core PCE decline — argues for at least a modest downward drift in next month's headline y/y, absent a rebound. I lean modestly toward a lower headline print next month, tempered by base-effect and monthly noise risk.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or core PCE y/y rebounding above 3.2% would reverse this call toward flat/up.
Leaned on: headline_mom, outgoing_mom, headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print holds at 3.90%, matching the prior three months — so the unemployment rate has plateaued rather than continued rising or reversing. My prior flat call was correct: the series did not breach 4.00% nor drop back to 3.70-3.80%, it simply stayed put at the recent high. That said, the broader trend context still matters: unrate_12m_low was 3.50%, and we're now 0.40pp above that low (sahm_gap +0.40pp), sitting at the top of the 12-month range (range_position 1.00). The 6m and 12m changes (+0.20pp, +0.30pp) show a gradual loosening drift over the past year, even though the most recent 3 months show zero net movement. The level itself (3.90%) is essentially at the 4% natural-rate benchmark, not stretched either way. With three consecutive flat prints at the cycle high, the signal is one of stabilization at a slightly elevated level rather than acceleration in either direction. Absent a fresh move, the most defensible call for the next report is flat, though the plateau at the range ceiling means any tick up would confirm renewed loosening while any tick down would suggest genuine improvement.
Would change its mind if: A print at 4.00%+ would confirm renewed loosening; a drop back to 3.70-3.80% would confirm genuine reversal rather than plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_3m_avg, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "up" call was partially right in trend (63d and 252d changes remain strongly positive, and the yield sits at 0.66 of its 12-month range) but the most recent leg has reversed: the last three prints (4.61→4.55→4.51) show a pullback, and dgs10_change_21d is now -0.12pp. This is exactly the falsifier condition I set (drop back below 4.55% with negative 21d momentum), though the 30s10s slope has not flattened — it's actually widened slightly to +0.14pp, which is a mild steepening rather than flattening, so the reversal signal is only partial. The level (4.51%) remains elevated versus the 12-month low (3.61%) and closer to the high (4.98%), so the medium-term uptrend in term premium is intact, but short-term momentum has clearly cooled. The Fed's announced slowing of Treasury runoff (cap cut from $60bn to $25bn) reduces net supply pressure from balance-sheet reduction, which should modestly ease upward pressure on long-end yields going forward. Given the conflicting signals — medium-term uptrend vs. short-term reversal plus reduced runoff pressure — I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-lower call for the next month, with low-to-moderate conviction given the mixed picture.
Would change its mind if: A renewed push above 4.60% with positive 21d momentum and further 30s10s steepening would revive the up case.

arbitrated.notes:
The two reaction-function drivers that actually matter for front-end pricing—labor and inflation—both came in essentially unchanged from last meeting: labor is a stabilized plateau at 3.90% (flat, conviction 0.35) and inflation is a modest-but-not-dramatic downdrift (down, conviction 0.35, based on a 16-day-old read with a soft 0.05% m/m print offset by hawkish-leaning Fed rhetoric about "lack of further progress"). Neither crossed the falsifier thresholds I set last time: no 4.00%+ jobs print, no CPI rebound above 0.35% m/m. So the core case for pricing slightly less near-term tightening survives, but it hasn't strengthened either—if anything the Fed's own language shift toward acknowledging "lack of further progress" tempers the disinflation read and argues against adding to the less-tightening trade.

Balance sheet continues to run off but the Fed has explicitly tapered the pace (Treasury cap $60B→$25B), which is a slow, well-telegraphed, largely-priced driver—I keep it as background support for term premium/duration debates, not as a lever on near-term policy pricing. Financial conditions remain in a stable accommodative plateau, giving the Fed no urgency to either ease or tighten faster; also non-actionable for the front end.

Given no material change in the two drivers that actually move front-end pricing, and no threshold breach in either direction, I hold the existing modest position rather than resize. The inflation call's slight softening in conviction (still down, but tempered by hawkish language) argues against adding risk; labor's plateau argues against removing it. Net: maintain a small bias toward less near-term tightening priced, expressed as a mild bull-flattener within the front end (long 2y vs short 3mo), unchanged in size and conviction.

discounted.why: Flat, stable plateau; well-anticipated, no near-term policy repricing signal.
Slow-moving, explicitly tapered and telegraphed; largely priced, belongs to duration/long-end debate.

arbitrated.falsifier: A jobs print at 4.00%+ confirming loosening, or a CPI m/m rebound above 0.35% confirming the Fed's 'lack of progress' language, would flip this back toward more tightening priced.

risks.text: Fed rhetoric shift toward 'lack of further progress' could presage a hawkish repricing even without new data.
Inflation call rests on a 16-day-old read; a fresh CPI print could reverse the down call quickly.
Position is small and low-conviction; noisy front-end moves could dominate the modest thesis.
Long 2y vs short 3mo carries negative carry if front-end stays inverted, eroding thesis if no repricing occurs soon.

trade.rationale: Labor and inflation both unchanged from last meeting, no falsifier triggered; hold existing mild bull-flattener expressing slightly less near-term tightening priced, unchanged in size.

why.labor_tightness: Unemployment plateaued at 3.90% for third straight month, no direction change; own-measurement is flat with slight loosening drift over 12m.
why.inflation: Headline/core still drifting down (CPI y/y 3.24, core PCE 3.01) but hawkish Fed language on 'lack of progress' tempers the down call vs last meeting.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI pinned at -0.41 for five straight weeks, no momentum either way; background constraint only.
why.balance_sheet: Runoff continues (assets falling) but pace explicitly tapered by policy; slow, priced driver, kept as constraint not lever.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.62
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: total assets fell again to 7,231,163 from 7,252,542, continuing an unbroken multi-week decline. The 4-week change (-53,156) and 13-week change (-253,576, -3.39%) confirm steady runoff, with assets now over $1.07T below the 52w high. The policy language explicitly continues QT, though it announces a slowing of pace: the Treasury redemption cap drops from $60B to $25B/month starting in [date] while the agency MBS/debt cap stays at $35B with excess reinvested into Treasuries. This is a deceleration of runoff, not a reversal — total assets will still decline, just more slowly going forward. Given the cap cut is roughly 60% reduction in the Treasury runoff pace, the actual monthly decline in fed_assets should shrink from recent ~$50-60B/4wk readings toward a lower rate, though MBS runoff and Treasury reinvestment-into-Treasuries provisions complicate the net pace. Directionally, assets remain firmly in runoff with no signal of pause or expansion. I maintain a "down" call but trim conviction to reflect that the shrinking cap will likely produce a smaller decline next month than the trailing pace suggests, and reinvestment mechanics add some uncertainty to the exact magnitude.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly rises in fed_assets, or a statement announcing pause/end of runoff/reinvestment exceeding caps into growth.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

  The previous "down" call was correct: headline CPI y/y fell to 2.97% from 3.24%, and the newest m/m print actually went negative (-0.04%), the softest in the 13-month series. Core PCE y/y also decelerated to 2.77% from 3.01%, continuing a steady downtrend and now within 0.77pp of target. The 3-month annualized headline pace (0.90%) is well below the trailing y/y rate, and the 3-month average m/m (0.07%) is far under the outgoing print, both signaling further disinflation momentum rather than a rebound. Core's 3m annualized (2.81%) is still a bit above target but consistent with continued gradual progress, and policy language upgraded from "lack of further progress" to "modest further progress," corroborating the data turn. None of my reversal triggers (m/m above 0.35% or core PCE y/y above 3.2%) were hit — in fact both moved further in the disinflationary direction. Given a negative m/m print and broad-based deceleration in both core and headline gauges, I expect headline CPI y/y to be flat-to-slightly-lower next month, though the very low base for m/m comparisons a year ago could produce some year-over-year noise in either direction, tempering confidence in a strong further decline.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print rebounding above 0.30% or core PCE y/y ticking back above 3.0% would reverse this toward flat/up.

Report:
  The latest print jumped to 4.10% from a plateau of 3.90% held over the prior four readings, which is exactly the loosening confirmation I flagged as my falsifier last time (4.00%+). This was not a one-off wobble: the 3m change (+0.20pp), 6m change (+0.30pp), and 12m change (+0.50pp) all point the same direction, and the rate is now sitting at the 12-month high, at the top of its range (range_position 1.00). The Sahm-rule-style gap (current vs 3m avg trough) is 0.47pp, uncomfortably close to the classic 0.5pp recession-signal threshold, indicating this is a rapid rise off the trailing low rather than noise. Distance from the ~4% natural rate is now +0.10pp, meaning we've crossed from tight into modestly loose territory. Policy language still describes job gains as "strong" and unemployment as "low," but the subtle rewording — risks "have moved toward better balance over the past year" (past tense) versus previously "are moving" (present, ongoing) — reads as an acknowledgment that the labor side has already shifted, consistent with the data. I was wrong to call flat last time; the plateau broke to the upside. Given acceleration and level both now unfavorable, I lean toward further softening next month, though one print is not yet a firm trend.
Would change its mind if: A pullback to 3.90% or below would suggest the 4.10% print was noise rather than a genuine trend break.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_high

The 30s10s slope is modestly positive (+0.15pp) and has ticked up, consistent with mild long-end steepening pressure rather than flight-to-quality flattening. The policy shift — slowing balance-sheet runoff by cutting the Treasury redemption cap from $60bn to $25bn — is a reduction in intended net Treasury supply pressure from the Fed's own portfolio, which is a modest disinflationary/dovish-for-duration signal for the long end (less runoff-driven supply hitting the market), arguing against a sharp continued rise. My prior flat call was roughly right in direction (yield only modestly higher, still within recent range), though the last print's move to 4.36 is a bit firmer than a pure flat reading would suggest. Net: slight upward drift in evidence but tempered by reduced QT pace working against it — I lean toward a small further increase but without strong conviction.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 4.20 with negative 21d momentum, or renewed 30s10s flattening, would flip this toward flat/down.

arbitrated.notes:
The key move since last meeting is labor: the falsifier I set myself (4.00%+ unemployment, confirming loosening) has actually fired — unrate jumped to 4.10% from a 3.90% plateau, with 3m/6m/12m changes all confirming, Sahm gap at 0.47pp, and the natural-rate distance flipping positive. That is a genuine trend break, not noise, and it directly undercuts my prior "labor_tightness +0.10" (leaning tighter) call. Labor is now the dominant reaction-function signal and it says loosening, which argues for less near-term tightening priced (front end should richen / yields down).

Inflation also confirms disinflation (CPI y/y down to 2.97%, negative m/m, core PCE decelerating, policy language upgraded to "modest further progress"), reinforcing the same direction — less tightening needed. My inflation call stays negative and if anything strengthens slightly given the confirmed miss on my own reversal trigger (m/m never rebounded above 0.35%).

Financial conditions and balance sheet are secondary, slow-moving constraints. FCI drifted mildly tighter but remains deeply accommodative in level — not enough to offset the labor/inflation combination. Balance sheet is still shrinking but the cap cut (60→25bn) is a deceleration of runoff, a marginal dovish-for-duration signal, but is a slow, well-anticipated variable with limited front-end repricing power.

Given labor has now decisively broken from tight to loose territory alongside continued disinflation, I'm strengthening the "less tightening priced" view versus last meeting rather than just holding it. I'll add modestly to the front-end richening trade, keeping it a modest 2y-vs-3mo bull-flattener-lite within the 2y ceiling, sized moderately since this is a one-month read and labor's move, while confirmed by multiple internal metrics, is still a single print.

arbitrated.notes:
Labor and inflation remain the two trade-relevant drivers here, and both continue in the direction I held last meeting, reinforcing rather than reversing the front-end call. Labor: unemployment rose again to 4.20% (from 4.10%), a fourth consecutive rise off the 3.70% low, with 3m/6m changes showing acceleration not stalling. This is genuine labor loosening, which argues for less near-term tightening priced (dovish for front end). Inflation: headline CPI y/y hit a fresh 12-month low (2.94%) and core PCE eased to 2.75%, with policy language upgraded to "modest further progress." The m/m uptick to 0.17% from -0.04% is noted but remains well below the 0.30-0.35% threshold I set as a reversal trigger, so this doesn't flip the disinflation read. Both reaction-function drivers now point the same way — cooling labor and cooling inflation — which is a clean, non-averaged case for less near-term tightening priced, not a case of resolving a tension between hot inflation and loose labor (that tension isn't live this month; both agree).

Financial conditions (modest tightening drift, -0.36) and balance sheet (continued runoff, decelerating pace) are backdrop confirmations of tightening constraints already priced/anticipated — slow-moving, largely known, and not what repriced yields this month. I hold them as context, not as trade drivers.

Given both labor and inflation confirm the prior thesis with no stale readings and no new tension, I maintain the position rather than resize it. The move in 10y (term premium down) is outside my mandate but consistent with a broader dovish repricing narrative. Falsifier holds from last time: unrate back to ~3.90% or CPI m/m above 0.30-0.35% would flip this.

arbitrated.notes: Labor and inflation are the two drivers that actually move the front end here, and both have softened relative to last meeting in a way that argues against my prior "more tightening" tilt. Labor: unemployment has now printed flat at 4.20% for two straight months, meeting the analyst's own stated falsifier for continued acceleration. The trend over 12m is still clearly toward looser labor (range_position 1.00, Sahm-gap territory, above natural-rate anchor), so I don't flip to a tightening (i.e., hawkish) read, but conviction that labor is still actively loosening in real time should come down — it looks like it's plateauing at a higher level rather than continuing to deteriorate month over month. Inflation: headline CPI's plunge to 2.61 was flagged as partly base-effects, and core PCE ticked up (2.75→2.81) for the first time in six months — the analyst itself moved from "down" to "flat," essentially neutralizing what had been my strongest driver for "less tightening priced." This is the key change from last meeting: the inflation call that anchored my -0.50 conviction has washed out to flat, and labor's forward momentum has stalled too. Financial conditions (mildly tightening, low conviction) and balance sheet (continued runoff, clearer signal but a slow-moving, well-priced constraint) don't independently reprice near-term policy expectations — they're context, not the near-term reaction-function signal. Net: the case for pricing MORE near-term tightening has faded, but so has the case for aggressively pricing LESS — both reaction-function drivers have moved toward neutral. I'm shrinking my "less tightening priced" position rather than holding it at prior size, since the evidence that drove it (falling inflation, accelerating labor loosening) has weakened materially this month.

discounted.why: Choppy, low-conviction, range-bound signal; not a near-term policy repricing driver
Slow-moving, well-anticipated QT pace already priced; constraint not a near-term trigger

arbitrated.falsifier: A CPI mom print below 0.05% or core PCE back below 2.7% (renewed disinflation), or unemployment dropping back to 4.00% (labor re-tightening), would reassert the 'less tightening' case; a third flat 4.20% print or hot CPI mom above 0.30% would flip toward more tightening.

risks.text: Core PCE inflection and labor plateau could both reverse quickly on next single data print, whipsawing the front end.
Reduced-size steepener still carries directional duration risk if labor data surprises loose again.
Holding a smaller short-duration tilt sacrifices carry if the front end stays anchored near current levels.

trade.rationale: Both reaction-function drivers (labor, inflation) have moved toward neutral from last meeting's clearer loosening/disinflation signal, so I halve the prior 'less near-term tightening' front-end position rather than holding or reversing it.

Assets remain well below the 52-week high (-875,723) and the 52-week change (-922,005) confirms a sustained, large-scale runoff trend with no signs of reversal. The statement language still affirms continued reduction of Treasury and agency MBS/debt holdings — the wording changed only stylistically (dropping "In addition"), not in substance; the commitment to runoff is unchanged and reaffirmed. There is no mention of tapering the pace, pausing, or ending runoff. Given the consistent weekly declines, no reversal signals, and reaffirmed policy language, I expect total assets to be lower again at next month-end, continuing at a similar or slightly faster pace than the recent trailing average.

Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was wrong: the spread continued its steepening trend for most of the window (0.06→0.26) before pulling back over the last two readings to 0.15. That said, this looks like a modest pullback within an established steepening trend rather than a reversal — slope_change_21d is still +0.15pp, slope_change_63d +0.44pp, and slope_change_252d +0.64pp, all positive. The curve sits at 0.86 of its 12-month range, near the top, well above the 12-month low of -0.53. The Fed's shift to cutting (50bp cut, easing bias language) supports continued front-end-led steepening as policy moves off restrictive levels, historically a steepening catalyst since the front end tends to fall faster than the long end initially. The two-observation dip (0.19→0.20→0.15) could be noise or partial retracement, but the broader multi-horizon momentum and the policy pivot toward cuts both argue for the steepening trend resuming rather than reversing. The 10s30s at +0.33 also shows a normal, healthily positive long-end slope, consistent with an un-inverted curve environment. I'll lean toward continued steepening (up) but temper conviction given the recent two-print softening.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.05 with slope_change_21d turning negative would signal renewed flattening and invalidate this call.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (up/tightening, 0.35 conviction) was wrong. The series has instead extended its easing trend: from -0.36 it moved to -0.37, -0.38, -0.39, -0.40, -0.41, -0.42 — a clear, monotonic decline over the last six readings with no reversal. The 4-week change (-0.04) and 13-week change (-0.03) both confirm negative momentum, and the index is now at -0.42, near its 52-week low (-0.44) and well below zero (distance -0.42), meaning conditions are looser than average and getting looser still. Range position (0.11 of 0-1) confirms we sit near the loose end of the past year's range. There is no sign of the stabilization or reversal that would have vindicated the prior tightening call — instead the brief uptick I flagged before proved to be noise, and the underlying trend of easing has resumed and strengthened. Given the consistency and monotonicity of the last six data points, I have reasonable confidence this easing continues into next month, absent any new shock. I'm revising to a down (easing) call with moderate-to-good conviction, correcting my prior misread.

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 10 readings show breakeven_10y drifting narrowly between 2.12 and 2.19, with the most recent print at 2.18 — near the top of this short window but still well inside the 12-month range (2.02–2.49, range position 0.34). The 21-day change is a modest +0.02pp, essentially flat, while the 63-day and 252-day changes are both negative (-0.14pp, -0.16pp), indicating the broader trend over the past quarter and year has been mildly disinflationary in market pricing even as the very recent tape has stabilized and ticked up slightly. My prior flat call was reasonably accurate: none of my trigger levels (sustained break below 2.10 or above 2.20) were hit, and the series has oscillated in a tight band around 2.15-2.18. There's no clear breakout signal — the recent uptick from 2.15 to 2.18 is within noise given the series' typical week-to-week variation of ~0.02-0.03pp. Breakevens remain 0.18pp above the 2% target, consistent with anchored but slightly elevated expectations. Absent a decisive move through either boundary, the most defensible call remains flat, though the short-term tilt is marginally upward given the last two prints.
Would change its mind if: A sustained break below 2.10 or above 2.20 over multiple readings would confirm a new directional trend.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Headline CPI y/y fell to 2.43% from 2.61%, extending a multi-month decline and hitting a new 12m low, with headline_yoy_change_3m at -0.54pp. However the outgoing monthly print (0.21%) rose from the prior 0.16% and 0.05% readings, and headline_3m_annualized (2.17%) sits below the latest y/y print, meaning further y/y deceleration is not obviously locked in — the mom trend has actually firmed slightly (mom_gap_vs_outgoing +0.04pp). My prior "flat" call was wrong in direction terms: headline y/y did fall further (to 2.43%), continuing the down trend I had discounted. More importantly, core PCE y/y ticked UP for the second straight month (2.75 → 2.81 → 2.87), a genuine reversal in momentum after months of steady decline, and core_yoy_change_3m is now +0.10pp (accelerating), not decelerating. Policy language also shifted to "somewhat elevated" (softer than "elevated") and "some further progress" (downgraded from "modest further progress" — actually an upgrade in the disinflation narrative, but core PCE's turn up complicates that. Given headline's recent monthly firming (0.21% vs 0.05% two months prior) and core PCE's clear upward inflection, I lean toward headline CPI ticking up or flat next month rather than continuing its glide down.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print back below 0.05% or renewed core PCE y/y decline below 2.75% would restore the down/flat disinflation call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print ticked down to 4.10% from 4.20%, breaking a two-month plateau at the cycle high. That partially validates my stated falsifier condition (a drop back toward 4.00% would signal stabilization). The 3-month change is now flat (+0.00pp), even though 6m (+0.20pp) and 12m (+0.40pp) changes still show a loosening trend over the medium term. The Sahm-gap of 0.47pp remains below the classic 0.50 recession-signal threshold, and the level itself (4.10%) is only modestly above the natural-rate proxy of 4.0%. Policy language shifted to acknowledge "job gains have moderated, and the unemployment rate has moved up," which is a soft acknowledgment of the drift already visible in the data, not a signal of acceleration. My prior "up" call was directionally right on trend but this specific print reversed - a modest pullback, not a continuation of monotonic increases. Given the flattening 3m trend, the tick-down from the recent high, and range position still elevated at 0.80, I read this as a pause/stabilization rather than a resumption of tightening or a fresh loosening leg. I lean flat with a slight upward bias given the 6/12m trend context, but conviction is low given the single-month reversal.
Would change its mind if: A renewed rise back to 4.20%+ or two consecutive higher prints would confirm resumed loosening rather than stabilization.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_12m_high, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call (conviction 0.45) has not been vindicated by the near-term trend: dgs10 rose modestly to 3.81 from 3.75, but the 21d change is still slightly negative (-0.06pp) and the 63d/252d changes remain sharply negative (-0.67, -0.80pp), indicating the dominant multi-month trend is still one of falling long-end yields, not rising term premium. The yield sits near the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.13, only 0.18pp above the 12m low of 3.63), which argues against a durable upward premium shift — this is a level consistent with continued anchoring rather than a breakout. The 10s30s slope is a modest 0.33pp, not indicating aggressive long-end cheapening. The last two prints (3.75→3.81) do show a short-term uptick, which keeps me from calling this outright down, but the broader momentum and range position dominate. Policy language is essentially unchanged substantively (same balance-sheet runoff continuation, just reworded), so no new signal there for term premium. On balance, the evidence is mixed: near-term uptick vs. persistent negative medium/long momentum and low range position. I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-up call with low-moderate conviction, acknowledging my prior thesis was too confident.
Would change its mind if: A clean break above 3.90 with positive 21d momentum and steepening 30s10s would confirm rising term premium; a drop back toward 3.70 would confirm down.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes:
Labor and inflation are the two drivers that actually move the front end, and both are giving a genuinely mixed but slightly less dovish signal than last meeting, not more. Labor ticked down to 4.10% from 4.20%, but the analyst reads this as stabilization/pause rather than a resumption of loosening — 3m change is now flat, and the medium-term (6m/12m) trend still shows gradual loosening. This is a soft, low-conviction read (0.25) that doesn't clearly resolve toward re-tightening or renewed loosening; I treat it as close to neutral with a very slight loosening lean given the 6/12m context, so I keep only a mild negative tilt.

Inflation is the more important shift: core PCE y/y has now turned up two straight months (2.75→2.81→2.87), and headline's monthly print firmed (0.21% vs 0.05% two months prior) even as y/y hit a new low. This is a genuine inflection in momentum, not noise, and it directly cuts against my prior "less tightening" carry. The analyst's own falsifier for my prior view (renewed disinflation) has not materialized — instead the opposite is happening on the underlying trend. Inflation expectations (breakevens) are flat/anchored, which tempers urgency but doesn't offset the core PCE inflection.

Financial conditions have eased more than expected (six straight looser prints, near 52w low) and balance sheet runoff continues at a steady-to-slightly-faster pace — both are slow-moving, already-priced constraints I weight lightly for near-term repricing purposes, though the FC easing modestly reduces urgency for additional near-term hikes-pricing.

Net: the case for pricing MORE near-term tightening has firmed modestly versus last meeting on the back of core inflation's inflection, while labor is roughly neutral-to-stable rather than loosening further. I trim my "less tightening" position — moving toward flatter/smaller front-end short, not reversing outright, since labor hasn't broken down and breakevens are anchored.

Policy language ("job gains have slowed... unemployment moved up but remains low") is consistent with this same plateau narrative, not a fresh acceleration. Absent a new leg up, the base case for next month is another flat-to-slightly-lower print, not a resumption of rising unemployment.
