arbitrated.notes: Since last meeting, the two reaction-function drivers I weight most both point mildly toward MORE near-term tightening pressure than I had priced, though neither is a clean break. Inflation: headline CPI mom reaccelerated for a second straight month (0.16→0.21→0.29), y/y rose to 2.58, and core PCE ticked up to 2.84 from a 2.75 low — this is the opposite of my flip condition (mom back below 0.10% or core PCE resuming decline) and instead confirms the "up" call with rising conviction (0.45 vs 0.40 prior). Labour: unemployment held at 4.10% for two straight prints after peaking at 4.20% — a plateau, not the renewed loosening my prior -0.10 conviction implied, and also not the 4.20%+ reinforcement that would flip me toward pricing more cuts. So labour is genuinely flat, not loosening, which removes the dovish leg I was leaning on. The net reaction-function read shifts from "modest less-tightening" to "roughly neutral-to-slightly-more-hawkish": inflation momentum is live and repricing-relevant, while labour has stopped confirming a loosening trend. Financial conditions (still easing, NFCI at 52w low) and balance sheet (still shrinking, mechanical QT) are the slow-moving constraints — largely priced, and per mandate treated as context, not drivers of the near-term repricing. I'm overriding my prior small easing lean: with labour no longer loosening and inflation reaccelerating, the front end should carry less easing priced in than before, meaning I trim/flip the front-end richness. This still stays within 2y, expressed as reduced pricing of near-term cuts (front-end yields modestly higher / smaller inversion vs bills).

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.75
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct and the trend has if anything accelerated. Total assets fell every single week across the 13-week window, with no counter-week at all. The 4-week change (-108,350) is larger in magnitude than one-third of the 13-week change annualized pace would suggest, indicating the runoff pace picked up rather than decelerated — consistent with continued balance-sheet normalization at or near the announced monthly caps. The 52-week change (-891,005) confirms a sustained, large-scale multi-quarter drawdown, and assets sit ~834,426 below the 52-week high with no sign of basing. Policy language remains unchanged: the Committee continues to reduce holdings of Treasuries, agency debt, and agency MBS, with no mention of tapering, pausing, or ending runoff. Given the steady weekly declines, an accelerating 4-week pace, and stable policy language reaffirming continued reduction, the highest-probability outcome for month end is another decline in total assets, roughly in line with or slightly larger than the recent 4-week pace (~$100-110B). No evidence of a policy pivot or reserve-scarcity concern is present. I hold conviction fairly high given the unanimity of the weekly declines and unchanged guidance.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly rises in fed_assets, or a statement announcing pause/end of runoff/reinvestment exceeding caps into growth.

arbitrated.notes: The reaction-function tension has sharpened since last meeting: labor_tightness now has unemployment pinned at the cycle high (4.20%, range_position 1.00), a genuine trigger of its own falsifier, and inflation has confirmed acceleration with both headline CPI y/y and core PCE y/y rising for a third straight month, mom prints running hot, and 3m annualized rates well above y/y — a clear upside momentum signal, not noise. Labor loosening and inflation firming simultaneously is the classic tension this seat is built to resolve. But the key asymmetry: near-term Fed reaction function is dominated by inflation persistence when labor is only gradually loosening from a tight base (4.20% is still low historically, Sahm gap modest) rather than breaking down. A single unemployment uptick at the top of a 13-month range is not yet a hard pivot signal for the Fed, especially with policy language merely softening confidence framing rather than shifting to easing bias. Inflation's momentum, on the other hand, directly raises the bar for near-term cuts and argues for less near-term easing being priced — reinforcing, not reversing, my prior stance. Financial conditions and balance sheet continue to ease/shrink respectively as expected, already well anticipated and priced, so I treat them as background confirmation rather than repricing catalysts. Net: modestly less near-term tightening priced by the market (i.e., current pricing of cuts should reverse somewhat) is not the right frame — I keep leaning toward less near-term easing being priced given inflation reacceleration, unrate not yet decisively breaking down. arbitrated.notes: The two reaction-function drivers that matter for the front end have diverged in a way that reinforces, not undercuts, my prior lean toward pricing more near-term tightening risk (or at least no near-term easing). Inflation: my prior falsifier (headline mom <0.15% or core PCE y/y <2.85%) was explicitly tested and failed to trigger — both CPI and core PCE printed hotter, headline y/y up three straight months, 3m-annualized running near 3.7%. This is a genuine strengthening of the "up" call, not a stale repeat, so conviction rises versus last meeting. Labor: the prior "up" (tightening) call did not confirm — unemployment ticked back to 4.10%, 3m/6m changes flat, and the analyst now calls it flat rather than continued loosening. This is a real update: labor is stabilizing, not loosening further, which removes one leg that might have argued for pricing out tightening. Net: inflation is the live, repricing-relevant driver here (data surprised hot, momentum building, Fed language downshifted from "further progress" to just "progress" — a hawkish tell), while labor has plateaued rather than genuinely loosened. That combination argues for maintaining a modest lean toward more near-term tightening priced, not less, consistent with holding rather than flipping my position. Financial conditions and balance sheet continue to ease/shrink respectively but both are slow-moving, already-anticipated series with limited marginal repricing power for 2y-and-in; I treat them as background constraints only, not the trigger for a position change. Curve/term-premium calls are out of my mandate (steepener bets, long-end). My falsifier condition was not met this month, so I hold the position with slightly firmer conviction given the inflation confirmation, tempered by labor's plateau rather than confirmation of renewed tightening.

My prior flat call was reasonably close but slightly stale: the last two readings (4.10 then 4.00) show a mild re-tightening trend rather than pure stasis. That said, this is a single month's move within a narrow 30bp band over the past year, and the level is right at the natural-rate estimate (4.0%), so this isn't a strong directional signal. Policy language still describes conditions as having "generally eased" earlier in the year, but the recent data don't confirm continued loosening — if anything they show a partial reversal. Given the small magnitude and single-month nature of the move, I lean toward flat-to-down for the next report, with modest conviction, favoring "flat" as the more conservative call given how narrow and noisy this series has been.
Would change its mind if: A rise back above 4.1-4.2% would confirm renewed loosening; a further drop below 3.9% would confirm clear re-tightening trend.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y last printed 4.58, up modestly from 4.52 two observations back but essentially flat over the last 21 trading days (change_21d ≈ 0.00pp). My prior "up" call was directionally consistent with the 63d and 252d trends (+0.30pp and +0.50pp respectively), and the level has not broken down toward 4.45 as my falsifier specified — instead it sits near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.82, only 0.95pp above the 12m low, 0.21pp below the 12m high). That argues the prior call was reasonably right in direction, though the very recent 21d flatness tempers near-term momentum. The 30s10s slope (+0.25pp) remains positive and has not flattened toward zero, and continued balance-sheet runoff keeps a structural bid against duration, consistent with mild upward pressure on term premium. With yields hovering near cycle highs and no clear catalyst for a reversal, but also no fresh acceleration higher, I lean toward continued modest upward drift or stability rather than a reversal, but conviction is capped by the flat 21-day reading.
Would change its mind if: A drop clearly below 4.45 with 30s10s flattening toward zero or negative would signal the upward pressure has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: Reaction-function drivers are the deciding vote here, and they are diverging in the direction I already flagged last meeting, but not enough to force a reversal. Inflation remains the highest-conviction, most trade-relevant read: headline CPI y/y up to 2.99, core PCE matching local highs, 3m-annualized momentum running well above y/y — a clean, un-stale reacceleration signal that argues against near-term easing and keeps a floor under front-end pricing. Labor is the tension: unemployment ticked down to 4.00% from 4.10%, a "flat-to-slightly-tightening" read at modest conviction (0.30) on a noisy single-month move within a 30bp range. That is a tightening signal (lower unemployment = tighter labor), which if anything reinforces the inflation call rather than offsetting it — there is no live loosening story to weigh against the hot inflation print. Financial conditions and balance sheet are both slow-moving, well-anticipated trends (NFCI grinding to a 52w low, reserves running off steadily) that are largely priced and don't reposition front-end policy expectations meaningfully month-to-month; I treat them as the constraint/background, not the trigger. Net: no new information here that flips the flip-conditions I set previously (mom CPI hasn't dropped below 0.15%, core PCE hasn't fallen under 2.85%, unemployment hasn't broken clearly below 4.0% — it's sitting right at it, not through it). The panel largely confirms last meeting's read rather than undercutting it, so I hold the position rather than resize. Slight nuance: labor's small re-tightening modestly reinforces conviction on "less near-term easing priced," so I keep the same trade with unchanged sizing.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.75
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The series continues its steady decline: newest value 6,766,101 vs prior 6,782,332, extending an unbroken downtrend across all 13 observations. The prior call (down, 0.68) was correct — assets fell further with no interruption. 4-week change is -52,085M, 13-week change is -139,039M (-2.01%), and 52-week change is -801,706M, all consistent with ongoing QT at a steady, undecelerated pace. There is no sign of the two-consecutive-weekly-rise falsifier, and policy language remains unchanged: the Committee continues reducing Treasury, agency debt, and agency MBS holdings, with no mention of pausing or slowing caps. The pace over the most recent 4 weeks (~52B) is roughly in line with the 13-week average pace (~139B/13 ≈ 10.7B/week vs latest weekly drop of ~15.8B), suggesting no deceleration signal yet, though a single week's move can be noisy. Given the consistent trend, unchanged reinvestment policy language, and no evidence of any pause, the balance sheet is very likely to continue shrinking into next month-end. This remains a clear, low-ambiguity continuation of trend rather than an inflection.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly rises in fed_assets, or a statement announcing pause/end of runoff/reinvestment exceeding caps into growth.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_change_52w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call has not played out well in the near term: slope_2s10s ended the last print at 0.25, still below the recent local peak of 0.27, and slope_change_21d is now -0.09pp, confirming the stall/reversal condition I set as my falsifier. That said, the picture is not a clean flattening trend either — the last observation (0.25) actually ticked up from 0.20 and 0.22 in the prior two prints, and slope_change_63d (+0.16) and slope_change_252d (+0.66) still show a solid multi-month steepening trajectory. The spread sits at 0.82 of its 12-month range (near the high end, 0.72pp above the 12m low of -0.47), so the curve remains historically un-inverted relative to its own recent history, but it's chopping in a fairly narrow band (0.20-0.27) over the last ten readings rather than trending cleanly. The Fed holding rates steady this meeting (after cutting previously) removes a fresh front-end steepening impulse, and 10s30s at +0.27 shows no acceleration in long-end pressure. Given the choppy, range-bound recent action with a modest short-term pullback but longer-term steepening still intact, I see this as close to flat with a slight residual steepening lean rather than a confident directional call.
Would change its mind if: A clean break above 0.27 with slope_change_21d turning positive again would revive the steepening case; a break below 0.20 would confirm flattening.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series bottomed at -0.51 and has ticked up over the last two readings to -0.49, giving a positive 4-week change (+0.01) despite still-negative 13-week change (-0.02). My prior "down" call was based on the easing trend continuing, but the latest two prints (-0.50, -0.49) show a reversal off the 52-week low, which is exactly the condition I flagged as a trigger to change my mind. The index remains deeply below zero (-0.49), so conditions are still much looser than average, but the momentum has clearly shifted: the series is no longer making new lows and is moving back toward its 13-week average (-0.49, essentially converged now) from below. This is a modest tightening impulse in an otherwise very loose regime. Range position (0.09 of 0.15 width) confirms we're still near the loose end of the recent range, so this is not a dramatic tightening, just a stall/reversal of the prior easing trend. Given the small magnitude of the uptick and the still-large negative distance from zero, I'd characterize this as a mild tightening lean rather than a strong one.
Would change its mind if: A drop back toward -0.51 (new 52w low) or 4-week change turning negative again would revive the easing-trend view.

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.20
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "up" call (0.35 conviction) has been contradicted: breakeven_10y actually declined from the 2.42-2.45 range down to a low of 2.36, and while the latest two prints (2.37, 2.38) show a small bounce, the level is still below where it stood a few readings ago. The 21-day change is -0.02pp, confirming near-term softening, even though 63d (+0.10) and 252d (+0.07) changes remain positive, reflecting the earlier run-up before the recent pullback. The series sits at 0.82 of its 12-month range (near the high), 0.38pp above the 2% target, so expectations remain elevated versus target but momentum has clearly stalled and reversed slightly over the last several weeks. The two most recent ticks (2.37→2.38) suggest stabilization rather than a continued slide, but not a resumption of the uptrend. Given the mixed signal — longer-term uptrend intact, short-term momentum negative, and a small recent stabilization — I see no strong directional edge for the next month. I am revising away from "up" toward a flat/mixed call, with low conviction, acknowledging the previous lean was not well supported by the most recent data.
Would change its mind if: A clear break below 2.35 for multiple readings would confirm downside; a push back above 2.42 would confirm renewed uptrend.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 13 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "up" call at 0.62 conviction is now contradicted by the newest data. Headline CPI y/y fell from 2.99 to 2.80, and the outgoing mom of 0.44 was replaced by a much softer 0.23 print — below my own 0.15-flip threshold band's implied deceleration signal, though not literally under 0.15, it's a clear drop from the prior trend (mom_gap_vs_outgoing -0.11pp). Core PCE y/y also dropped to 2.78 from 2.99, breaching my stated 2.85 flip level. So both of my falsifier conditions have essentially been met: headline momentum decelerated sharply and core PCE fell below 2.85. The 3-month annualized core figure (2.46%) is now much closer to target than the y/y level suggests, and headline 3m annualized (4.04%) is still elevated but driven by volatile components given core-minus-headline is near zero currently. Policy language also softened, dropping the "progress toward" framing and simply stating inflation "remains somewhat elevated" — arguably a slightly less alarmed framing removal, ambiguous but not hawkish. Given the sharp single-month drop in both headitem and core, I was wrong last time to lean up. The momentum now points toward disinflation continuing, though one month of data could partly reflect noise. I'll shift to a down lean with moderate conviction, watching for confirmation.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print rebounding above ~0.35% or core PCE y/y rising back above 2.95% would flip this back toward up/flat.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 20 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print jumped to 4.20% from 4.00%, matching the cycle high and reversing last month's dip. My prior "flat" call was wrong in direction for this print — the +0.20pp move was a real bounce, not noise, though it's within the recent 3.9-4.2% range that has held for many months. Looking at the full series, the rate has been oscillating in a tight 3.9-4.2% band with no clear trend for a year — 4.1, 4.2, 4.2, 4.1, 4.1, 4.2, 4.1, 4.0, 4.2. The 3m and 6m changes are literally 0.00pp, meaning this is choppy sideways movement rather than a sustained rise. The 12m change of +0.30pp and sahm_gap of +0.20pp show mild cumulative loosening from the low, but the level remains only modestly above the ~4% natural rate estimate. Policy language shifted to describing the market as "stabilized at a low level" and "solid," which argues against reading this print as the start of a fresh deterioration trend — it looks more like normal month-to-month noise within a stable band. Given the range-bound history, I lean toward the next reading reverting toward the 4.0-4.1% area rather than extending the rise, but the recent jump raises some risk of continued upward drift.
Would change its mind if: A second consecutive print above 4.2% would confirm a genuine break of the range and a real loosening trend rather than noise.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.35) was wrong: the 10-year has declined from 4.47 to 4.24 over the last 10 readings, with the 21-day change at -0.31pp, clearly breaching the 4.45 threshold I set as my own falsifier. The trend is a steady grind lower, not a reversal-then-bounce; the most recent print (4.24) is the lowest in the series. Range position sits at 0.53, mid-range, so the level itself isn't extreme, but the recent momentum is clearly downward. The 30s10s slope at +0.27pp has not flattened to zero/negative, so the long-end curve isn't screaming distress, but it also isn't confirming renewed upward premium pressure. Balance-sheet runoff continues per the policy language, which is a structural upward force on term premium, but it has been overwhelmed by whatever is driving yields down recently (likely flight-to-quality or other cross-asset flows outside my coverage). Given the clear, sustained -0.31pp move over 21 days versus a flatter 63-day and 252-day change, I read this as a fresh downward move in the long end rather than noise. I'm revising to a lean-down call, with moderate conviction given the offsetting structural runoff pressure.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above 4.40 with 30s10s steepening further would suggest the downward move was noise, not a new trend.

  inflation_expectations lacks inflation — Realized CPI trend would clarify whether breakeven pullback reflects genuine disinflation or noise.

  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Would clarify if broader risk-off/flight-to-quality flows are driving the yield decline.
  term_premium lacks inflation_expectations — Falling breakevens vs falling real yields imply different term-premium dynamics.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel has moved materially against my prior read. Last meeting I leaned on a hot inflation print (+0.62) plus a labor market I saw as only modestly tight (+0.15), carrying a small net-hawkish front-end position (long DGS2, short DGS3MO). This meeting, inflation has flipped: headline CPI y/y fell 2.99→2.80, core PCE y/y broke below my own 2.85 flip-threshold to 2.78, and mom decelerated sharply (0.44→0.23). That is a clean falsification of my prior inflation call, not noise — both of my stated flip conditions were essentially met. Labor is the offsetting force: unemployment jumped to 4.20% (cycle high), a real bounce, though still within a year-long 3.9-4.2% range with 3m/6m change flat at 0.00pp. The labor analyst itself calls this likely noise/reversion, not a trend break, and wants confirmation via a second consecutive 4.2%+ print before committing to a loosening call. So I have a confirmed inflation downshift against an unconfirmed, possibly-noisy labor uptick.

Given the front-end reaction function is asymmetric to confirmed data, I weight the inflation reversal more heavily than the tentative labor bounce. Financial conditions ticking up modestly (mild tightening off a loose base) and balance sheet continuing to shrink at a steady, priced-in pace are structural/slow-moving and don't override this near-term repricing — they're the constraint, not the driver. Net: the case for pricing in *more* near-term tightening has weakened notably since last meeting; the case for less has strengthened. I'm cutting my net-hawkish front-end tilt significantly, moving toward flat-to-slightly-dovish, but not flipping hard short-rates given labor's ambiguity and the single-month nature of the CPI drop.

discounted.why: Slow-moving, near-loose-end reversal too small to reprice near-term path meaningfully.
Steady, well-anticipated QT pace; structural background, not a near-term repricing driver.

arbitrated.falsifier: A rebound in headline CPI mom above ~0.35% or core PCE back above 2.95%, or a second consecutive unemployment print at/above 4.2%, would flip this back toward more tightening priced.

risks.text: Fed language only softened marginally ('somewhat elevated'), not a confirmed dovish pivot; one-month CPI drop could be noise.
Labor uptick could be the start of a genuine loosening trend the labor analyst itself flags as a risk.
Front-end richening if inflation data reverses next month would leave this position exposed to a snapback.
Reduced hawkish tilt gives up carry if near-term cuts don't materialize as fast as implied.

trade.rationale: Confirmed inflation reversal offsets prior hawkish tilt, but unconfirmed/noisy labor bounce and Fed's only mildly softer language argue against a confident directional front-end bet yet. Move to flat pending confirmation.

The main new policy input — slowing the runoff pace (cutting the redemption cap from $25bn to $5bn) — reduces the pace of Treasury supply hitting the market via balance-sheet runoff, which is a modest disinflationary-for-term-premium force (less net supply pressure), reinforcing rather than reversing the recent downward drift. Waller's dissent preferring the faster runoff pace is a minor hawkish counter-note but doesn't change the operative policy. With the trend intact, no break above 4.40, and reduced runoff-driven supply pressure, I lean toward continued softness or stabilization in the term premium, though the pace of decline has decelerated (21d change is small), suggesting the move may be flattening out near current levels.
Would change its mind if: A rise back above 4.35-4.40 combined with renewed 30s10s steepening would indicate the down-trend has reversed into fresh term-premium pressure.
Leaned on: dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s, dgs10_level

Your previous meeting

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: total assets fell again over the last 4 weeks (-36,033 $M) and 13 weeks (-92,857 $M), continuing a steady runoff trend that has taken the balance sheet 585,730 $M below its 52-week high. However, the policy language shows a material deceleration ahead: effective [date] the Treasury redemption cap drops from $25B/month to $5B/month, while the agency MBS/debt cap stays at $35B. This is a substantial slowing of the pace of QT (potential total cap runoff falls from up to $60B/month to as low as $40B/month, though actual paydowns are often below caps). The direction remains runoff (down) — assets should still decline into month-end — but the recent 4-week and 13-week pace (~$9-30B/month) already looks close to or even below the new combined cap ceiling, so continued decline is likely but at a decidedly slower rate than the trailing 52-week pace (-611,075 $M) would suggest. I maintain a "down" call for next month-end, but with reduced conviction given the explicit policy shift toward a much slower runoff pace, and note Waller's dissent shows the taper itself was contested, underscoring this is a genuine regime change, not just noise.
Would change its mind if: A weekly rise in fed_assets, or evidence that actual Treasury+MBS runoff has flattened to near-zero under the new $5B/$35B caps.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.40) played out modestly: the spread ticked from 0.51 to 0.52, a small further steepening rather than reversal, even though the falsifier condition (drop below 0.50 with negative 21d change) was partially triggered—slope_change_21d is indeed -0.05pp, but the level held at/above 0.51 throughout and closed at 0.52, not below 0.50. So the flattening signal was weak/mixed, not a clean reversal.
  
  Looking at the full picture: the 12-month trend remains strongly steepening (252d change +0.99pp), the spread sits near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.87), and the very recent 21d wobble (-0.05pp) is minor noise against that backdrop, with 63d change still solidly positive (+0.27pp). The last four prints (0.51, 0.51, 0.51, 0.52) show stabilization after the mid-series high of 0.58, not a breakdown. The curve remains meaningfully un-inverted, well above flat (+0.52pp) and off its 12-month low. Balance-sheet policy language (slowing redemption caps) is a modest technical support for longer-duration steepening pressure but is unchanged in direction from prior guidance.
  
  Given the plateau near range highs with only a marginal recent pullback, I lean toward continued mild steepening but with reduced conviction versus last time, since the near-term momentum has clearly stalled compared to the strong 63d/252d trends.
Would change its mind if: A break below 0.48 with slope_change_21d turning more negative (below -0.10pp) would confirm a genuine flattening reversal, not just stalling.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_distance_from_flat

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up/tightening, 0.40) was wrong: the series continued easing after the interim peak. From the recent trough of -0.38 the index has fallen for four straight readings (-0.38, -0.39, -0.40, -0.42, -0.44, -0.45), a clear renewed easing trend, exactly the reversal condition I flagged as my falsifier. The 4-week change is now -0.06 (easing), while the 13-week change is only +0.04, reflecting the mid-window bounce that has since fully reversed. Conditions remain well below zero (-0.45), i.e., looser than historical average, and are now closer to the 52-week low (-0.51) than the high (-0.36), with range position at 0.36 and falling. Momentum is the dominant signal here: three to four consecutive weekly declines outweigh the stale 13-week average. With no policy language contradicting this and no offsetting evidence, I read this as a resumption of the easing trend that had only paused mid-series. I am revising toward down (easing further) for month-end, with moderate conviction given the streak is short and could stall again as it did before.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above -0.40 with a positive 4-week change would indicate the easing trend has stalled or reversed into tightening again.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_above_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was wrong: breakeven_10y has stabilized and ticked back up, from 2.32 to 2.34 in the last observation, with be_change_21d now +0.11pp — a clear reversal of the deceleration I had flagged. The 63-day change is only mildly negative (-0.04pp) and the 252-day change is flat-to-slightly-positive (+0.02pp), suggesting the recent dip was noise within a range rather than a trend down. Levels remain elevated relative to the 12-month range: be_range_position at 0.73 shows breakeven sits in the upper third of its year-long band, and distance from the 2% target is a still-meaningful +0.34pp. The last two readings (2.32, 2.32, 2.34) show a modest but real uptick after several months of gentle softening. Given the acceleration in the 21-day change and the proximity to the 12-month high (2.46) rather than the low (2.02), the more consistent read now is that inflation expectations are stabilizing or drifting mildly higher, not continuing to fall. I do not have strong conviction this is a sustained re-acceleration, but the flat-to-down thesis from last month is no longer well supported by the most recent print.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.32 sustained for multiple readings, or be_change_21d turning negative again, would revert this to a down/flat call.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release (newest headline_cpi_yoy = 2.38%) rose slightly from 2.33% the prior month, and my flat call was roughly correct — the move was small and within noise. Looking at the full picture now: headline y/y sits at 2.38%, near the bottom of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.08, only 0.05pp above the 12m low), and headline_yoy_change_3m is -0.42pp, showing clear deceleration over the past quarter. The most recent mom print (0.10%) is below the 3m average (0.10% avg is actually equal, but well below the 0.43% peak seen a few months back), and headline_3m_annualized is just 1.18%, well under target-consistent pace. Core PCE has also cooled — 2.61% latest vs 2.99% three months prior, a -0.17pp drift down, though it remains 0.61pp above the 2% target and above headline (core_minus_headline +0.24pp), consistent with somewhat elevated underlying pressure. Policy language shifted to explicitly flagging inflation risk alongside employment risk, but this is a balance-of-risks statement, not evidence of re-acceleration. Given the deceleration trend in both headline 3m annualized and core y/y, plus the low mom print, I lean toward continued softness or a flat-to-slightly-lower headline print next month, not a rebound.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print above 0.30% or core PCE y/y rebounding above 2.9% would signal renewed acceleration, invalidating this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_mom, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.30%, a new 12-month high, up from 4.20% and confirming the loosening scenario I flagged as my falsifier last time. My prior flat call at 0.40 conviction was wrong: the rate did not stabilize at 4.1-4.2 as it had for months, it broke higher. The 3m change is now +0.10pp with the level moving to the top of its 12-month range (unrate_range_position = 1.00), and the 12m change is +0.40pp, a nontrivial cumulative drift away from the 4.0% recent low. Sahm-gap of 0.23pp is still below the classic 0.5pp recession-signal threshold, so this isn't a sharp acceleration, but it's not noise either — it's a second consecutive move to a fresh high after months of a tight 4.0-4.2 band. The policy language shift is notable: from "stabilized at a low level" to explicitly flagging "risks of higher unemployment... have risen," suggesting the committee itself sees the trend as tilting toward loosening rather than range-bound stability. Given the level is now 0.3pp above the 4% natural-rate anchor and the trend has broken out of its prior band, I lean toward continued modest loosening over the next month, though the magnitude is still modest.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 4.2% would suggest this was noise; a print at/above 4.4% would confirm accelerating loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_high, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (4.41) is down slightly from the recent local peak of 4.58 but still sits well above the 4.35-4.40 zone I flagged as my reversal trigger, and the 12-month range position (0.67) shows yields in the upper third of the past year's range. My prior "down" call was wrong in direction over the last month: dgs10_change_21d is +0.24pp and change_63d is +0.17pp, both showing a net rise, not a decline, even though the very latest few observations have ticked down modestly (4.47→4.43→4.41). The 30s10s slope remains elevated at +0.51pp, consistent with continued long-end steepening pressure rather than compression, which typically signals rising term premium. The policy language reinforces this: the Fed is slowing the pace of balance-sheet runoff (redemption cap cut from $25bn to $5bn), which is a modest disinflationary-for-term-premium force (less supply pressure from the Fed side), but Waller's dissent preferring to continue the faster runoff signals some committee tension and the slowing is not a reversal of runoff, just deceleration. Net: yields have oscillated but remain elevated versus 12-month lows, slope is steep, and the recent pullback is minor. I lean toward marginal upward or flat, not a resumption of the downtrend.
Would change its mind if: A sustained break below 4.35 combined with 30s10s flattening would confirm renewed term-premium compression and validate a down call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes:
Labor is the driver that has actually moved since last meeting: unemployment printed a fresh 12-month high at 4.30%, breaking the 4.0-4.2 band that had held for months, with range_position at 1.00 and the Fed's own language shifting to flag "risks of higher unemployment... have risen." This is exactly the falsifier-adjacent evidence I flagged last time (a print confirming a new high), though it landed at 4.30% not above 4.2% as I framed it — still, the direction is unambiguous loosening, and labor is the highest-weight driver for front-end pricing. Inflation, by contrast, is stale (16 days old) and still reads flat/softening: headline decelerating, core PCE cooling to 2.61% from 2.99%, no rebound signal. The competing inflation_expectations read (breakeven ticking up) is a market-based, slower-moving signal I weight less than the direct CPI/PCE trend for near-term Fed reaction. Financial conditions have resumed easing (four straight weekly declines) and balance sheet runoff is decelerating (Treasury cap cut from $25B to $5B) — both are constraint-context, already well-anticipated, and shouldn't override the reaction-function signal.

Net effect: labor loosening + inflation cooling + financial conditions easing all point the same direction — less near-term tightening pressure, arguably building a case for earlier/more cuts being priced. This actually reinforces my prior position rather than reversing it. The labor print is the key update: it confirms the loosening thesis more strongly than last month, while inflation offers no pushback. I maintain the front-end richening bias, keeping DGS1 heavier (most sensitive to policy path over the next few meetings) against a small DGS3MO offset, with modestly higher conviction given the labor confirmation.

discounted.why: Treated as slow-moving constraint context, not a reaction-function trigger for near-term policy path.
Well-anticipated, slow-moving; policy shift already flagged, doesn't reprice near-term path materially.

arbitrated.notes: The two reaction-function drivers I actually own for the front end have both reversed relative to last meeting, and the reversal matters more than the outright levels. Labor: my prior "tightening" read (+0.50) was explicitly premised on continued unemployment rise; the new print (4.10%, down from 4.30%) undershoots even my own stated noise threshold and the analyst now calls flat/loosening-risk with weak conviction. That is exactly the "labor print reverting to 4.1-4.2%" falsifier I set last time, and it has fired. Inflation: headline CPI reaccelerated (2.68% y/y, mom 0.25% vs 0.10%) and core PCE ticked to 2.78%, both moving up though neither cleared my stated rebound trigger (0.30% mom / 2.9% core). Still, direction is up, not flat, and that's the opposite of what I carried last time (-0.25 inflation conviction). So both drivers that matter for near-term Fed pricing have flipped sign versus my prior read: labor loosened (less tightening pressure), inflation firmed (more tightening pressure) — a genuine tension rather than a one-sided move. Net reaction-function signal is roughly neutral-to-slightly-hawkish rather than the dovish tilt I carried. Financial conditions and balance sheet continue to ease/shrink respectively, but these are the slow, largely-priced constraint variables per my mandate — supportive of the front end staying anchored, not drivers of repricing. Given labor has genuinely reversed and inflation has firmed, I no longer have basis for the same size dovish front-end bet. I trim the position meaningfully toward flatter/smaller rather than holding the prior conviction, since the core evidence underpinning it flipped.

arbitrated.notes: Labor and inflation are the two reaction-function drivers that matter for the front end, and both have actually moved since last meeting in ways that partially unwind my prior dovish lean. Labor: unemployment hit 4.30%, the top of the 12-month range, confirming the loosening I flagged last time but only assigned -0.15 conviction to — this is now a live, confirmed signal, not noise, and argues for less near-term tightening being priced (dovish for front end). Inflation: headline CPI continues to firm (2.74% yoy, mom 0.23%) but the analyst's own read is that the 3m-annualized pace (2.35%) is below the yoy print, meaning the increase is largely base-effects roll-off rather than fresh momentum — genuinely equivocal, not the clean reacceleration my prior +0.40 conviction implied. Core PCE ticked up but is mid-range with no trend. So the inflation upside case has weakened somewhat versus last meeting, while the labor loosening case has firmed and cleared its own falsifier threshold (4.30%). Financial conditions and balance sheet continue their slow, well-anticipated grinds (looser FCI, continued QT) — both largely priced and not repricing near-term policy expectations; I treat them as background constraint, not signal, per mandate. Net: the balance of reaction-function evidence has shifted slightly more dovish than last meeting — labor loosening is now confirmed at range-high with clear momentum, while inflation's up-call is real but weaker/more equivocal than before. This supports modestly increasing the dovish tilt on the front end rather than holding flat, but not aggressively given inflation is still nominally "up" and core PCE remains elevated. I trim the DGS1 short-rate long slightly and keep the small 3mo/1y structure, reflecting a marginally larger discount for near-term tightening.

A hot jobs report could reverse the labor loosening confirmation within one print given only 0.30pp range width.
Front-end is already pricing meaningful cuts; further dovish repricing may be limited without a clearer catalyst.

trade.rationale: Confirmed labor loosening (4.30% range-high) modestly outweighs softened inflation up-call; slightly deepen DGS1 dovish tilt while holding small 3mo anchor leg unchanged from prior.

  The position you are carrying: DGS1 -0.25, DGS3MO +0.10 (conviction 0.35).
  You said this read would flip if: A labor print back below 4.10% (loosening reversal) or headline CPI mom above 0.30%/core PCE above 2.9% (clear reacceleration) would flip this back toward less dovish or neutral.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: assets fell again in the newest observation (6,603,384 from 6,618,415), a drop of roughly $25B in the latest week alone, continuing an unbroken run of declines over the last several weeks. The 4-week change is -$39,194M and 13-week change is -$69,860M, both negative, with the run-rate actually accelerating in the most recent two prints (-$25,231M and -$15,031M) versus the flatter pace seen a few weeks earlier. Assets are now $511.6B below the 52-week high, and the 13-week average continues to be pulled down by fresh lows. The policy language confirms continued runoff of Treasury and agency MBS holdings under the existing caps, consistent with the observed decline. There is no evidence of a pause, taper announcement, or reversal — the trend is monotonic runoff with no weekly rises to trigger a change of view. Given the consistency and even slight acceleration of the decline, I maintain a down call with somewhat higher conviction than last time, since the recent weekly drops are larger than the trailing average pace, reinforcing that QT is proceeding uninterrupted into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A weekly rise in fed_assets, or confirmation that actual runoff has flattened to near-zero under current caps.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was wrong: the spread has moved from the 0.55-0.60 area up to 0.64pp, near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.96, only 0.03pp below the 12m high of 0.67). The condition I set for reversing my view — a rebound with slope_change_21d turning positive — has been met (21d change +0.21pp, 63d +0.12pp, 252d +0.73pp), so the steepening trend that had stalled has clearly resumed. The last three prints (0.65, 0.60, 0.64) show some chop but the level is decisively higher than a month or a quarter ago, and the curve is now solidly disinverted at the 2s10s point (distance from flat +0.64pp). Waller's dissent for a 25bp cut, layered onto an unchanged statement, points to easing bias building at the front end, which historically favors further steepening as front-end yields anchor or fall while the long end holds. With the spread parked near its 12-month high and momentum still positive across all three lookback windows, the path of least resistance is further modest steepening, though the extreme range position raises some risk of consolidation or a pause near current highs.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.55pp with slope_change_21d turning negative would signal the steepening move has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_distance_from_flat

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has flattened at its 52-week low. The last four readings are all -0.52, with nfci_change_4w at only -0.01 and nfci_change_13w at -0.07, indicating the easing trend that dominated the prior three months has essentially stalled. Conditions remain well below the zero average (-0.52 distance from zero), so financial conditions are still looser than historical norms, but the momentum that would justify calling further easing is gone — the index is pinned at its range floor (range_position 0.00) with no fresh downward push. My prior call of "down" was directionally consistent with the trend into this print, but the last four flat prints show that trend has run its course for now. Absent new data or policy language, the most defensible call is that the index sits essentially unchanged into month-end: the flattening at the 52-week low suggests inertia rather than a new leg of easing or a reversal into tightening. There's no evidence of a reversal (would need a move back above -0.45 with positive 4w change), but there's also no evidence of continued easing given the plateau.
Would change its mind if: A move back above -0.45 with a flat-to-positive 4-week change would signal reversal; a fresh break below -0.52 would signal renewed easing.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.35) was wrong: the series did not roll over. From the previous readings (ending near 2.41-2.42) breakevens have held flat-to-firm, printing 2.43 then settling at 2.41, 2.41. The 21d change is +0.02pp (essentially flat, not negative as would have confirmed my prior thesis), while 63d (+0.07) and 252d (+0.26) both remain solidly positive, showing the medium-term trend is still up. Levels sit near the top of the 12-month range (range_position 0.89, only 0.05pp below the 12m high of 2.46), and the distance from the 2% target is a full 0.41pp — a meaningful, persistent overshoot in market-implied expectations. The last two prints are identical at 2.41, suggesting the recent modest pullback from 2.43 has stabilized rather than extended into a new downtrend. Given the flat very-short-term momentum but firmly positive medium/long-term trend and a range position near cycle highs, I read this as inflation expectations holding at an elevated plateau rather than resuming a decline. I do not see enough deceleration to call this down again, but the flattening in the last few prints argues against calling a strong renewed uptrend either.
Would change its mind if: A break below 2.39 sustained for two readings would confirm renewed downtrend; a push above 2.44 sustained would confirm resumed uptrend.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y rose from 2.74 to 2.94, near the top of the 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.92), and the newest monthly print (0.35% m/m) came in above the 3-month average (0.28%) and well above my stated falsifier threshold of 0.15%. Momentum is building, not fading. Headline 3m annualized (3.37%) now runs meaningfully above the y/y rate, signaling further upward pressure into the next release. Core PCE also ticked up (2.81→2.86) though its 3m annualized (2.99%) is more moderate, and core_yoy_change_3m (+0.25pp) is smaller than headline's (+0.56pp), suggesting some of the recent headline pop is coming from more volatile components (energy/food) rather than pure persistence. Still, both series are moving the same direction, and headline is the metric being scored here. Given the strong recent m/m print, the acceleration in 3-month annualized headline inflation, and the position near the top of the recent range, I expect headline CPI y/y to rise again or at least hold near this elevated level at the next release, with modest risk of a pause given core's more tempered momentum.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.15% or a pullback in headline y/y below 2.7% would signal the acceleration is stalling.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print held at 4.30%, matching the prior month rather than climbing further or reverting to 4.10%. My previous call (up, 0.35) anticipated continued drift higher; instead the rate plateaued at the cycle high. The series has been choppy (4.20→4.10→4.10→4.20→4.10→4.00→4.20→4.20→4.20→4.30→4.10→4.30→4.30), but the recent trend is unmistakably toward the top of the 12-month range: unrate_range_position is at 1.00 (sitting exactly at the 12-month high), sahm_gap is a modest +0.23pp, and unrate_above_12m_low is +0.30pp off the trailing low. The 3m change is flat (+0.00pp), but 6m and 12m changes are both mildly positive (+0.10pp each), suggesting a slow grind higher rather than sharp deterioration. This is not yet Sahm-rule territory (gap would need ~0.50pp), but two consecutive prints at the cycle high after a sequence of sub-4.20 readings tilts the signal toward continued softening rather than mean-reversion. I'll maintain a modest upward lean, tempered by the fact that the rate did not break into new high ground this month — a genuine plateau, not accelerating loosening.
Would change its mind if: A print back at/below 4.10% would confirm mean-reversion; a print above 4.30% would confirm accelerating loosening and raise conviction sharply.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_12m_high, unrate_above_12m_low

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.35) was wrong: yields have drifted lower, not higher, since then. The 10-year sits at 4.23, near the middle of its 12-month range (position 0.52), well off the 4.79 high and only 0.60pp above the 4.34 recent print, and now within the 4.22-4.34 band shown in the last 10 readings. Both the 21-day (-0.14pp) and 63-day (-0.18pp) changes point down, even though the 252-day change remains positive (+0.41pp), showing the longer trend is still up from a year ago but the recent momentum has clearly reversed. The 30s10s slope at 0.69pp is fairly steep, not flattening aggressively, but has not signaled fresh upward pressure either — my flattening threshold for a bearish reversal (0.40 or below) has not been hit, yet the price action itself has already moved the wrong way from my prior call. Ongoing balance-sheet runoff is a background upward force on term premium, but it is being outweighed for now by whatever is driving the recent declines (likely softer growth/inflation expectations or flight-to-quality, outside my mandate). Given the clear short-term downward momentum and mid-range positioning, I now lean toward continued softness or at least stabilization near current levels rather than a renewed rise.
Would change its mind if: A break above 4.35 with steepening 30s10s toward 0.80+ would indicate renewed upward pressure on the long-end premium; a drop below 4.15 would confirm downside continuation.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: The two drivers that actually matter for front-end pricing — labor and inflation — have moved apart from last meeting's read, and the resolution favors staying on the "less near-term easing priced" side, though with slightly tempered conviction than before. Inflation strengthened further: headline CPI y/y at 2.94 near 12-month highs, 3m annualized running at 3.37% (above the y/y rate), and core PCE also ticking up, all above my prior falsifier threshold of 0.30% mom. This is a live, un-stale read (16 days old) confirming reacceleration, not just persistence — a clear case for the reaction function staying hawkish/on-hold. Labor, meanwhile, plateaued rather than continued loosening: unemployment held at 4.30% (cycle high) for a second month, not breaking higher into accelerating-loosening territory nor mean-reverting back toward 4.10%. This is a flattening of the loosening signal, not a reversal — my prior "up" (loosening) conviction was leaning on continued drift, and that drift stalled. The panel shows labor and inflation now pulling in different directions on the tightening question: inflation says less cutting warranted, labor plateau is neutral-to-mildly-loosening but not accelerating. Given the Fed's reaction function currently weights inflation stickiness heavily (per my mandate to resolve tension rather than average), I keep leaning toward less near-term easing being priced, i.e., front-end yields should not fall much further and may need to hold or firm slightly. Financial conditions have flattened (no longer easing) and balance sheet continues its steady runoff — both consistent with, not fighting, this read, and I treat them as background confirmation rather than drivers of the call. I maintain my existing modest front-end position with slightly reduced size given labor's plateau tempers the strength of the tightening case somewhat.

why.balance_sheet: QT runoff continuing and slightly accelerating; steady background tightening force, not a near-term repricing driver.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35

  My prior call (down, 0.65) was wrong on the near-term print: the last three weekly readings show a modest but consistent rebound (6602071 → 6605962 → 6608597 → 6608395), and the 4-week change has flipped positive (+5011 $M) after months of decline. This is a small move against a backdrop of the 13w change still negative (-53805 $M) and the 52w change deeply negative (-471664 $M), so the multi-month QT trend remains intact and the level sits well below its 52w high (-438530 $M). However, the policy language still explicitly commits to continued runoff of Treasury and MBS holdings, which is inconsistent with sustained balance-sheet growth. The most plausible read is that weekly noise (settlement timing, coupon reinvestment lumpiness, or a temporary slowdown in redemptions) is producing a short-lived uptick rather than a genuine policy shift, since no language change accompanies it. Given the explicit continuation of runoff guidance, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for the next month-end print, but the recent 4-week reversal materially lowers my confidence in a clean "down" call versus my last release.

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous call (up, 0.45) leaned on continued steepening, but the falsifier I set has now triggered: the spread printed 0.56, just barely above the 0.55 threshold, after touching 0.52 two prints back, and slope_change_21d is -0.08pp (negative), confirming the near-term momentum has stalled/reversed from the prior steepening impulse. The last observation (0.56) is a modest bounce off the recent low, but the series has been choppy in a 0.52-0.59 band for the last several prints with no clear trend — essentially oscillating rather than trending. Longer lookbacks still show steepening (63d +0.08, 252d +0.30) and slope_range_position at 0.83 shows the spread sits high in its 12-month range, so the broad trend since a year ago is toward steeper/less inverted. But the immediate 21-day signal is flattening, and the rate cut just delivered (with a dissent for a larger cut) suggests front-end rates falling further could still pull 2s10s wider — but that's a slope_slope_curve judgment, and current data doesn't show sequential confirmation. Given the choppiness and conflicting near vs long-term signals, I see this as close to flat with a slight tilt toward the recent stalling pattern rather than confident continuation of steepening.
Would change its mind if: A clean break above 0.60pp with slope_change_21d turning positive would confirm renewed steepening; a break below 0.50pp would confirm flattening resumption.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has been essentially pinned at -0.52 for the last seven readings after a gradual slide from -0.49. The 4-week change is flat (+0.00) and the index sits at its 52-week low, with range position near 0.01 (bottom of its recent band). This confirms the prior flat call was accurate: momentum has fully stalled after the earlier easing trend, with no fresh break lower or reversal higher. Conditions remain looser than the historical average (distance from zero -0.52), but there is no directional impulse currently — the series has plateaued rather than continuing to ease or beginning to tighten. Absent any new move in either direction, and with the latest policy statement silent on this driver, the most defensible call for month-end is unchanged. The flatness across seven consecutive prints is unusually persistent, which lowers the odds of a sudden move but also means any small wiggle could tip the reading marginally either way. arbitrated.notes: The panel largely reaffirms last meeting's tension rather than resolving it, but the labor read has strengthened further against my prior "up" lean while inflation has confirmed hot. Labor: unemployment printed 4.40%, a fresh 12-month high, up from 4.30%, with the analyst's own falsifier threshold (4.30%) triggered and conviction raised to 0.45 on continued loosening. This is unambiguous: labor is loosening, which argues for LESS near-term tightening priced (and even opens room for cuts to be pulled forward), directly opposing my prior small "hawkish" tilt on labor. Inflation: headline CPI momentum remains firm (3.02% y/y, 0.30% m/m, top of 12-month range), confirming the "up" call with conviction 0.55 — reaction function still has genuine hawkish input here. This is the classic labor-loosens/inflation-hot tension the mandate flags. Given the Fed's reaction function has shifted more dovish on labor language in recent cycles once unemployment breaks trend and hits multi-month highs, and given the labor move is now confirmed (not just directional) while inflation's acceleration pace is described as "leveling rather than steepening," I judge labor as the more live, repricing-relevant driver for the front end right now. Inflation expectations (breakevens) ticking down modestly reinforces that the market itself sees less inflation urgency. Financial conditions and balance sheet are flat/noisy and not directional near-term catalysts — properly treated as constraints, not movers. Net: I now lean toward the market pricing somewhat LESS near-term tightening than my prior stance implied, given confirmed labor loosening outweighs a "leveling" inflation acceleration. This is a partial reversal, not a full one — inflation still argues against aggressive easing pricing, so I moderate rather than flip outright, trimming the short-yield-up tilt I was carrying.

Momentum measures are mixed: headline_3m_annualized (3.43%) still runs hotter than the y/y print, suggesting some upward drift risk, but core_3m_annualized (2.40%) is decelerating and core y/y has actually ticked down 0.08pp over three months, with the latest print (2.83) below the recent local peak (2.91). Core running below headline (core_minus_headline -0.17pp) and decelerating is the more important signal for persistence, and it argues against imminent reacceleration in headline. Net: headline is near a local plateau at an elevated level (0.8-1.0pp above target on core), with recent momentum roughly flat-to-slightly-up on headline but cooling on core. I don't see enough evidence for a confident directional break either way — most likely essentially unchanged next print, perhaps a marginal uptick given the 3m annualized skew.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or headline y/y above 3.15% would signal renewed acceleration; a drop below 2.85% y/y would confirm deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print fell to 4.40% from 4.50%, a decline of 0.10pp — this is the "below 4.60% acceleration threshold" and close to my stated stabilization marker (4.30% or below), though it didn't quite reach that level. My prior "up" call was directionally too aggressive for this print; the rate actually ticked down last month rather than continuing to rise. That said, the broader trend over 12 months is still up (+0.20pp), the 3-month change is still positive (+0.10pp), and the level remains 0.40pp above the 12-month low and 0.40pp above the natural rate estimate, with the Sahm gap holding at 0.43pp — closer to conventional recession-signal territory than to full stabilization. Policy language changes are notable: officials now describe downside employment risks as having "risen in recent months" (upgraded from a static risk assessment), and note "more recent indicators are consistent with" the slowing/upward-edging narrative — suggesting they see continued softening beneath the single-month dip. Given the single-month decline could be noise around a still-elevated and drifting-up base, and the policy tone leans toward further loosening, I lean toward another modest uptick or at least sideways-to-up next report, but with reduced conviction since the immediate print surprised to the downside.
Would change its mind if: A print at or below 4.30% would confirm stabilization/reversal; a print above 4.60% would confirm renewed sharp acceleration.
Leaned on: unrate_change_12m, unrate_change_3m, sahm_gap, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y has drifted sideways-to-slightly-up over the last month, closing at 4.18%, matching its recent high in this window. My prior "down" call was wrong in direction (though small in magnitude): the 21d change is now +0.09pp and the yield sits at the top of its recent range, not breaking lower toward 3.95 as I'd flagged. The 12m range position (0.26) shows yield still in the lower third of its 12-month band, but the short-term momentum is modestly up. The 30s10s slope at 0.66pp has not cleared my 0.70 steepening threshold, so the strongest upside trigger isn't confirmed, but it's closer than before. Policy language has shifted meaningfully: the Fed ending balance-sheet runoff and now signaling it will conduct purchases of shorter-term Treasuries to maintain reserve levels is a mild net negative for long-end term premium pressure (removes some supply overhang from runoff, and purchases are concentrated short-end, so less direct long-end support). This is a mixed signal — modest upward yield drift on measurements, offset by ballast from the end of QT/runoff. Net: modest, low-conviction lean toward continued slight upward drift or flat, not the down call I made last time.
Would change its mind if: A break below 4.05 with 30s10s flattening toward 0.55 would confirm renewed downward term-premium pressure; a clean push above 4.25 with slope above 0.70 confirms upside.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Details on pace/size of new short-term Treasury purchases would clarify net duration supply impact on long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment would clarify if flight-to-quality is suppressing or not suppressing long yields.

arbitrated.notes:
Labor and inflation — the two drivers that actually move front-end pricing — both softened relative to last meeting's read, and this matters more than the balance-sheet story. Labor: the unemployment rate actually ticked DOWN to 4.40% from 4.50%, which is a tightening in labor-market terms (lower unemployment = tighter labor), missing the analyst's own stabilization marker (4.30%) only narrowly, and against a policy backdrop where the Fed explicitly upgraded its downside employment risk language. The analyst still calls "up" (rate rising over time) but with reduced conviction (0.30) and flags this print as a genuine surprise against the trend. Inflation is unambiguously flat: headline plateaued at 3.00% y/y, core is decelerating (2.83%, down from 2.91% peak), and 3m annualized core (2.40%) is well-behaved. Inflation expectations also flipped from "down" to "flat," with the breakeven uptick not yet confirmed as a trend reversal. Neither inflation reading supports re-acceleration or additional tightening pricing.

The net reaction-function signal has shifted mildly dovish/loosening since last meeting: unemployment fell (tighter labor in the classic sense, but the Fed's own language stresses downside employment risk, not overheating), and inflation is flat-to-cooling on core. This argues for less near-term tightening priced, not more — a continuation and modest reinforcement of my prior short-end-richening bias (lower yields at the very front, curve favoring lower rates near-term), consistent with the existing position. Balance sheet expansion (QT ending, shorter-bill purchases) is a slow-moving, largely-anticipated constraint variable — priced in via policy language already — and doesn't override the reaction-function read. Financial conditions are flat/pinned at loose levels, non-signal. I hold the position rather than resize since no driver flipped decisively against last meeting's read; if anything the case for less tightening firmed slightly on inflation persistence-of-cooling and dovish Fed language, but not enough to add size given labor's own surprise miss.

discounted.why: Flat, low-volatility regime at floor; no momentum, doesn't move front-end pricing.
Slow-moving, policy-language-confirmed and already priced; constraint not near-term reaction-function driver.

arbitrated.falsifier: A labor print at/below 4.30% confirming stabilization, or headline CPI m/m above 0.35%/y/y above 3.15% signaling reacceleration, would flip back toward pricing more near-term tightening.

risks.text: Fed's dovish employment-risk language could reverse quickly if a hot jobs print arrives before next meeting.
Balance-sheet expansion (short-bill purchases) could richen front-end more than modeled, understating the long side of the trade.
Front-end richening is a crowded trade after months of dovish repricing; any inflation surprise could unwind fast.

trade.rationale: Hold existing front-end richening tilt (long DGS1 yield down vs small short DGS3MO offset): labor and inflation both point to less near-term tightening, but labor's own-measurement surprise keeps conviction unchanged rather than increased.

why.labor_tightness: Unemployment ticked down to 4.40% (tighter), missing analyst's own stabilization threshold; keep modest up-lean but low conviction given surprise.
why.inflation: Headline plateaued, core decelerating; essentially flat, marginal upward lean only from 3m annualized skew.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI pinned in tight 0.02 band at loose extreme; no new signal, treat as non-mover for front end.
why.balance_sheet: Weekly assets clearly turning up plus explicit policy language confirming QT end and short-bill purchases; slow-moving, already-anticipated constraint.
